entries.text: Workshop: 1911 kurtzmann: stripped grand piano down to the soundboard, removed all bolts and screws, agraphs, and i also polished them. I took the dampers out, cleaned them. I took the plate out, sanded the soundboard and repaired minor cracks with epoxy glue. Waiting for further instructions on Monday.

Ludwig & Co.: casters replaced, key bed out, planning take strings measurements and plate out next week. 
Backshop: I assembled the kholer & Campbell,  the kimball and the stritch (I made some modifications in the fall board)

Bauer #14660: I made progress on the bridge recapping. Steinway #208427: tuned. 
I did not get to the schimmel #1121 because I am working on learning some new tuning technique which currently makes me slower. 
Thank you for making this internship possible! I really enjoyed it and learned so much!

Lester 66823 Removed screws for plate and moved plate to proper placed 
Steamed off action felt 
Replaced felt on action bar
Baking soda blasted action
Removed saddles and old hammers
Removed brass butts from old hammers and installed them onto the new hammers. I found out that I needed to re-pin each of the hammer butts with a different size pinning and learned proper swing testing. Re-glued each of the Jack Falanges. After apppying to all 88 I had 8 that did not line up properly. I learned why and re-glued those 8 with proper alignment.

Howard 179443: Finished up cleaning and polishing. I glued the right side of the wooded rim around the plate, needs to be looked at by Julio.                                                           Kroger 41368: Ivory and I cleaned all the dust out, replaced felt on balance and front rail, cleaned keybed, polished strings and scrubbed plate, polished all plate screws and hardware, polished pedals, replaced felt and cleaned throughout trapwork, and refurbished and polished all parts of the action. (action just needs strong rail put on as well as rest of bridle straps, other than that it's a basically all done.)                                           Edit: we did not do the dampers yet
Finished the Yamaha u3, along with the hailun 121 293288 and hailun 121 253930

Cleaning is done on the Kranich and Bach. Just got a start on the action prep. I changed the string that was kinked. The neighbor string broke while I was trying to get a tight coil, so I ended up changing two strings.
Admin; Krakauer 17724: Started regulation, will probably take me until next Thurs. or Fri. to complete.
Hardman 93975: Keys are done and in key bed. Miller 51003: Keys are done and in key bed. Steinway 315614: Keys are done and en route to owner. Mason & Hamelin 34240: Korbin will start these keys.

Bush and lane- 17827
I finished replacing the bottom board and verified that the screw holes matched. I recounted all of the bottom board hardware after cleaning and polishing them.
I replaced the castors saving the old castors in a zip lock bag.
I them moved on to the pin board. I cleaned and polished the board as well as each pin.
I then moved on to measuring the strings. I completed the bass string, and am currently working on the others.
Colby-30776
I also measured the bsss strings on this piano because I was on the same step and wanted more practice.

Lyon & Healy 25539: I built the keys that needed it. I glued on their key tops. I had to reglue some of the keys due to the key tops not making full contact with the front of the keys. I got the keys flush. I am currently working on the sharps. The piano is in Julio‘s shop. So once it’s out, I will finish them. I haven’t started on the Chickering yet. But, I will next week once I finish the sharps on this key set.

Kroeger 41368: Avery and I thoroughly cleaned out all the dust from within and the back of the piano. polished the strings, tuning pins, plate screws and pedals. refurbished all parts of the action and replaced felt wherever necessary. removed old dampers. all that's left is to install new dampers, finish putting on the bridle straps and installing the spring rail.

All warranty work completed. White 148 Finished and tuned. Yamaha C3: I'm sorry,  but I got distracted with the player install. Would you give me 3 hours to finish regulation, Pitch raise and tune it? Hailun 178 QRS: Coming along quite well! Got the rail mounted and the action is prepped and done. All that is left is the trapwork for the pedal solenoid, driver boards installed and all the wiring done. Should be two and a half days to three probably.
Winter 323395: done Starr 76199: git-r-dun in progress, keys tops milled off, sharps removed, bushings steamed out
Knabe 128695: respaced strings, removed dampers and glueing on new dampers. Baldwin 412826: respaced strings, some felt seems cut from strings

Conway  78344  Base bridge cap flush trimmed, installed and aligned new hammer butts; reglued whippen jack flanges, aligned 90 degrees and with back check; repinned a few; replaced let off buttons and felt; replaced felt on rest rail, spring rail; scotch bright rest rail wood stained touch up; attached bridal straps; scotch bright damper rod buffed and reinstalled.

Steinway 214896: took out all cabinetry and stored upstairs. Cleaned trap board and buffed all screws and exposed metal hardware and replaced felt. Soda blasted key bed and polished each pin. Sanded over stubborn and uneven spots. Replaced front and back punchings (waiting on a few more green ones that have been ordered). Waiting on felt for back rail. Removed bass strings.

Farrand. Completed hanging hammers. Had to cut my go bass dampers for fit. Have leveled, squared and spaced keys. Will have to fine tune after keytop replacement is finished. Have completed capstan height and adjusted rest rail. Treble section let off completed. I think I'll have it completed by Wednesday.
Gulbransen: I have glued 2 out the 3 stacks and cleaned some pieces In the times while the glue was drying. I also got the motor to work and I serviced the motor.
Brewster 180017 : Doing Buushings, almost ready for final file   Melanie Cambell Keys Nearly Done

Petrof 557404: Removed all castles and were polished on the buffer. Mason & Hamlin 11572: removed dampers with steam method, soda blasted action, removed hammers, kept section ends, bolstered hammer butts, it’s a brass rail so I didn’t repin them, cut off hammer shanks, bored hammer butts, replaced bridle straps, tightened all action screws, reglued all jack flanges, reinstalled all hammer butts, replaced some of the tiny brass plates on the rail, replaced a broken damper flange. I think I will be done on Monday. I only have to replace let off button felts, rest rail cloth and lubricate the action.

Broadwood 62561 - dampers are as good as I can get them, everything else is completely finished except for two notes that have jacks that keep clicking and the key spacing issue I talked about previously.  Steinway 64233 - Many whippens replaced, new dampers have been installed and fully completed. Spring rail is missing 4 springs

Tunings 6 out of 7, did a warranty repair, Baldwin Acrosonic 784967: some repairs on the action, leveled keys, eased keys, backchecks, all I have left is let-off/lost motion/aftertouch, and add some felt so the pedals don't clunk against the top, and then I'll be done.
#72873 Chickering - Continued redoing the hammers that Brigham requested, and began redoing the dampers as well.

Cable 402514: finished regulation, fixed misshaped hammers. I will regulate dampers on Monday. I noticed that the second to last sharp towards 88 is slanted (they weren’t replaced by us) I just left it because I didn’t want to remove the action again today but if you want I can take care of that on Monday. 

Tunings rescheduled: 2/3

Ludwig & Co.: bass strings ordered, measurements done, plate out.  
Backshop: I helped translating Curtis for Doris. 
I’m wondering if you got my email about de refinishing shop. 

Photos: 54523 Grinnell Bros (before) // 214896 Steinway (after) // 287448 Hailun (after) // 1293074 Wurlitzer (before) // 27802 Heintzman (before) // 10149 Zwicki (before) // 90128 Steck aeolian duo art (before) // 116388 Knabe (before)

Su: Got M&H 22167 stuff out, cleaned and prepped damper heads. M: Started gluing damper felts, got trained on QB, prepped field kit, did H&C T10078 QC, prepped Knabe with pounder, helped Evan and Garret with removing plates. Tu: Knabe needed butt leather and felt replaced, warranty and tuning went well. W: Knabe 143274 regulation was completely awful, fixing damper timing ruined spoons and spoons could only be adjusted with the action out. All that's left is aftertouch and then I'm calling it. The showroom work needs to be rescheduled, I had no time today and with the Kimball + warranty I won't have time tomorrow. I'll try to get it all in Friday. Th: Finished Knabe, trained Luke on string measuring/teardown, warranty with Matthew.
Benches: assembled enough benches to have one at each piano in the showroom. put nicer benches with grands. Helped steve replace the springs on the springrail.

Replaced all let off buttons and let off eye screws 
Installed let off rail  
Repaired damaged damper hinge 
Buffed damper rods 
Lubricated with CSP and teflon
Shined and cleaned rest rail (I already replaced the felt here previously per the instruction I received.)
Removed thin action felt and prepared the felt for gluing
Saddled the hammers 
Replaced 5 damaged saddles 
Sanded and cleaned spring board and glued new felt on.
Sanded and cleaned the action’s metal frames.
Began full rebuild on Steinway 55038.
Removed cabinetry making sure there was a good amount of foam between already finished pieces. 
Labeled and bagged screws.
Removed and numbered keys. **special note- the keys that existed there already had had the number  order switched around. The numbers I penciled in are as they were found in the piano.**
Removed action and placed on storage shelf. Tipped piano and removed bottom board. 
Sanded bottom board and removed and buffed metal pieces.
Replaced petal felts around levers.
Glued cracks in bottom board.
Removed old casters and installed new ones. Piano is level with new casters. Removed piano legs and prepared to remove keybed. 
Soda blasted, and cleaned keybed. Ordered new felt for the back of the keybed. It does not arrive for another 2 weeks. Measured strings and cut old ones out, saving the bass strings. Removed tuning pins and saved 3. also be aware both capo bars were cracked in half and will most likely needed to be welded together. I placed both capo bars with their broken pieces taped to each other in my piano box. Removed plate. **note that both sides of the piano must be removed before plate can be placed in or taken out.** cleaned piano front and back and began upload of tech photos. Steamed off action dampers and removed hammer rest rail.
Ivory and I worked on all the same parts. His report should show what we did.
Finished prepping/ regulating all the pianos on my schedule

Bedding the keyframe, key heights, aligning hammers to strings (some strings to hammers), whippen traveling/alignment & centering Jack in the window are all finished. Remaining regulation, tuning, and assembly is left. I'll be coming in some extra hours this next week to make sure the piano is done for  the delivery.

Finished hanging hammers. Finished leveling and dip on keys. Started traveling hammers as well as keeping them squared and mostly aligned. Stripped off most paint off of the plate. Tuned all 5 pianos, as well as fixed the noise in the mahogany 1P

Finished the Kranich and Bach on the 9th. The action took a bit of work and took longer partly because of the different design. The Sterling is in the middle of cleaning. I put the bass strings in your office. All measurements have been made. The keybed is ready to come out on Monday. I'm hoping the action prep will be super fast with the new hammer butts.

Admin, Krakauer 17724: completed regulation and trapwork was already done, so I finished up on Wednesday! Started restringing the Stroud 87215 and will most likely be done with that by Wed./Thurs. then I'll move on to regulation.

I completed measuring the treble strings  and removed them. I then went on to remove the plate. there were 2 screws that were pinned by the wood and the plate was unremovable with them still in, so I cut into the bottom a bit in order to remove them. I was then told that given the configuration of my plate that I would need to remove a side of the piano.One side was very loose, so I removed that side with no difficulty.  as I was removing the plate screws, one of the screws closest to the right side snapped. I was told that in order to extract that screw I would need to remove the right side of the piano as well. Using a hammer and wedges I removed that side and the back of the piano was strapped to a wall to prevent it from falling. I then cleaned my area and moved on to the action. I removed felt using steam and then removed the damper board. I soda blasted the action making spacial care to do it at a distance that would bot cause lines in the wood. I removed the other two bars and the hammers. I replaced the hammer buts and had a bit of trouble when doing the butt alignment but after a day I was given the ok to move to the next step. I am Currently reglueing the jack flanges. I noticed that several were not quite aligned so I removed them before the glue could set. I will rescue them and work on the rest on monday.

Chickering 109841: I soda blasted, stamped, removed the natural and sharp key tops and put the naturals through router one. The key fronts are the weird ones with the curve at the bottom so I manually removed the fronts. Glued new natural key tops on. I touched up some of the sharps. I scored the chunks and pieces that come off of some of the sharp key sticks and glued them on. I got a lot of keys flush. I need probably 2-3 more days. Lyon & Healy 25539: I got the natural keys flush.

Kroeger 41368: Cleaning and Action Prep has been completed. The Action has been installed and the piano is ready for next steps.   Yamaha G5 1168615: The Plate has been installed and stringing cloths have been replicated.   Francis Bacon 241008: Thorough cleaning on the inside has been completed. The back still needs to be cleaned. The Action Prep has been started, all hammers have been removed and Action has been sodablasted. Currently working on bolstering the hammer butts. I am not sure which parts will need to be re-plated (pedals, hinges, knobs, etc.) please check and advise so we can put these parts aside.
No Showroom items this week, but the player and the square grand is DONE! :D

End o' the Week Notes: Starr 76199: done, there were 4 keys completely cracked in half, fixed them, but it would be nice to have someone else double check them. Samick HHA 02278: replaced two chipped keys. Could probably use a bit o' leveling to match the height of the older more experienced keys. Bush & Lane 28077: in progress, old keys milled off, bushings steamed out, currently drying with sizing cauls in. Baldwin 2 key replace: done Areli Davis/Dennis?: (not sure exactly what this is called), in progress, soda blasted, numbered, salvaged ivory,
Knabe 128965: glued dampers, dampers aligned, hammers drilled, cove done, tapered, and whippens hanew whippens have been put into the action, spaced and roughly adjusted for the hammers

Merrill 38180: completed upright teardown: cleaning and prep at the hybrid level. Includes steps 1-21. Disassembled cabinetry, removed metal hardware, took brass to replating room, cleaned key bed, shined strings, shined tuning pins, cleaned the plate, cleaned the trap work, buffed and sanded plate screws and bolts, shined capo bar, buffed action holders and knobs, replaced and leveled casters,  cleaned back of piano, checked for metal trash. Moving on to upright action prep, completed steps 1-11. Cut off dampers with steam, removed hammer rest rail, soda blasted, unhooked bridle straps, numbered and removed hammers, and bolstered hammers.

Steinway 54630: Completed Teardown, Trapwork restoration, Keybed restoration, String and pin removal, Screws/bolts and plate removal, Soundboard cleaned, Casters installed, Plate screws and bolts sanded, Dampers removed, Action soda blasted.

Colby 30776 De strung the piano took all measurements. Removed all screws and bold in the plate including a side block that was in the way. Mapped all the screws and bolts. Plate is ready to be removed first thing

Tuning: 10 Beautiful pianos. I am quite pleased with how each of them sound. Knabe 48335 Cleaning. Most of the hardware is buffed and cleaned up. The Trapwork is likely to be reassembled on Monday. Thank you for the awesome week! I sure enjoyed it throughly!
Farrand. Struggling a little bit with dampers. Just need to patiently get a few right. after removing and dropping the action, I'll need to re-regulate a bit. I'll get going on stringing G5.

Gulbransen: all the decks are finished and installed, but as I'm assembling the piano I have noticed that a few functions of the piano were disabled by the previous technician who had worked on this piano. I'm also waiting on the cloth we had ordered on new years, Victoria said they had been shipped so I'm hoping they arrive on Sunday.
Brewster:180017 Needs Final filing when julio is done with the piano Kohler and Campbell is done Started sanding Broadwood Keys

Mason hamlin 11572: replaced rest rail cloth and let off button rail felts. Whitney 86701:   removed the external parts, numbered and removed the keys and the action, stored on a shelf. Removed the Key bed  frame and the old felts, keeped samples, took it outside to sodablast, sanded the dirtiest parts and finally cleaned with TPS. Cleaned the front pins y balance rail pins with scotch brite. Removed every plate screw, bolts and metal structures and polished. Polished Capo bar with bright boy. Made the caster holes bigger with the chisel  and replaced all casters. Removed the trap work and sodablasted, screws and pedal bar  polished in the buffer, replaced the felts and leather. I put the trap work back in place. I noticed that the front casters need shims so I’ll take care of that on Monday. I will start action prep on Monday.
All QCs, tunings, etc. are finished.

Steinway 64233 - Dampers straightened, new hammers installed. I fixed several issues with jacks, and I found that some of them are longer than others. I may have to take them all off and cut the longer ones down to match.

Tuning = 5 out of 7, did a warranty repair which took awhile, but I did upsell another tuning, which she will schedule soon, Baldwin Acrosonic 784967: finished the regulation, but I still need a little more time with it, there's an awful buzzing with some of the bass notes I'd like to take care of,
#72873 Chickering - Nearly finished aligning the damper heads. Glad to be back, and looking forward to finshing the dampers and hammers next week!

Cable 402514: regulated dampers. Done! 

Schafer & Sons 6072606: replaced fallboard felt, worked on the end sections, made let off sooner, gave more drop, made sure al hammers were checking at a pianissimo. 

Gulbransen 191466: installed keybed, put action in the piano and none of the action bolts lined up so I fixed that. The traveling was awful so I fixed that. It’s missing a whippen and sticker so I’ll take care if that on Monday or Tuesday. 

Tunings rescheduled: 3/3
Strich 4261: cleaned, only the letters on the metal piece that was painted gold need to be painted. Ludwig & Co: still working on action prep

M: Showroom work + tuning, not scheduled out. Tu: Showroom, M&H 22167, Draper warranty was a tight flange, and tuning was not scheduled out. W: M&H is at a standstill due to missing pedals, waiting on response from Baris and owner before continuing with a The Shop Provides plan. Started on Taylor 23325, balance rail needed to be routed down in the keyframe to get keys low enough, leveling to start tomorrow. Th: More taylor keybed work, key easing, and key leveling, warranty. F: Finished leveling, started regulation, did the damper guide rail and squeaky pedal in the showroom. The taylor needs about two days to finish regulation and trapwork if no more major issues pop up.

finished replacing the springs for steve (except for the last few because we ran out.) Straube 38187 soda-blasted action started cleaning. Straube 38187 filed cabinetry, cut off old hammers, made hammerbutt jig to dril broken shanks.

Removed hammer butts, dampers, and wippins and gave info for them to be ordered. 
Buffed up the brass parts of the action with bright boy. 
Replaced the spring board felt.
Put everything away for the Steinway as we could make no more progress until new parts arrive for the action. (Customer wanted to specially request we replace these parts of the action). I spoke with Curtis about what to do with the remaining time of the day and he taught me how to secure the sound board to the ribs and had me work on the Ellington piano 52031. Curtis also had me remove the long piano bridge as the separation was happening right by and underneath it. (I was taught this is not normally the case.) After allowing 24hr of the glue drying for the anchors I made I filled in the holes with glue and hammer shanks. All except for 2 screws were replaced with this because I found another spot that needed to be glued, it will need 24hrs until it’s ready to be replaced with a hammer shank. Brigham communicated with me that he wanted me to work on the Steinway piano sound board. Finished gluing Steinway ribs and confirmed completion of ribs by Matthew and Jake. Began work on Wester 33045. Had to find appropriate shelving. Removed cabinetry. Found 1918 news papers folded under the keys of the piano. I placed these in a ziplock bag and have them to Melissa. Removed action and vacuumed the keybed. Removed old casters. Most of the old casters were stripped out or had broken off screws. Drilled out old casters and removed damaged screws, and began installing new casters. Removed bottom board and glued crack, and sanded down, replacing old felts. Removed and cleaned keybed. Measured bass and treble strings filling out paper work and removed strings. I had to fully remove the sides. Of the piano to get the plate out. Removed back screen cover and prepared piano for cleaning.
Finished the hailun 1p, hailun 120, Hd 118, and started regulating the kawai 235431

Chickering is done & delivered. I've spent some time today cleaning up, putting away the table I had been using, throwing out old parts, & organizing white boxes in the back storage room. I've started on the Conway and have most of the work left. I've aligned & spaced the damper heads on the treble section.

Finished all the tunings, replaced 5 strings on the HD 1899330 and HD 1900849. Finished stripping off paint on the Baldwin plate. Finished traveling hammers as well. On the kimball I finished installing the dampers. And then I am now almost done tightening the strings (which I wish I had noticed before I installed the dampers).

Sterling: Finished up the Sterling action. I put the rest rail and spring rail back in to keep things together and put the action in the cabinetry room. Thompson: Took apart and took keys out and vacuumed. It was great working here again! I hope to return!

Admin and: Finished restringing the Stroud 87215, glued dampers in, regulated the damper lift and made some modifications to the spring rail to give the dampers room to lift. It's still a little tight on the last few of the treble dampers, so I might need to shim more off the spring rail to take care of that. I should be able to start hammers on Monday/Tuesday and be done by the end of the week. If I finish before week's end, then I'll start on regulation. I've started using a stop watch on my phone when I do tech work to keep better track of time spent on shop work and it works really well. This week I spent 14.5 hours on shop work, so we're still not quite at a 50/50 balance with admin. work, but not sure how to make the balance better.

Bush and lane 17827. I finished breaking and reglueing the flanges, I believe that this had been done recently  because the glue was so strung most of the time the wood would break before the glue gave way, so I had to do repairs on about 7/10 of each flange. no felt was needed on the whippens so I moved on to replacing the let off buttons. I then installed the letof rail and lubricated everything  before I cleaned and replaced the felt for both the rest rail and the spring rail.  I pit my action back into storage and clean up my area before I moved on to the-  zeck91821- I started to travel the hammer buts when I noticed that most of them already had a substantial amount of travel paper behind them. I checked to see if they were straight, and they were not. si removed the hammer butts and blew out all of the old travel paper to start fresh. This time I added the butts a few at a time and had a much easier time straightening them. I will likely do it that way in the future when possible.  the last thing I did this week was start breaking the flange glue on the zeck.  The glue on this one is much older and more brittle and I have yet to break a flange.

Sohmer Grand 139803: I did and got a lot done this week.  I removed the key tops, built up the two keys that I routed down. Glued on new key tops, got them flush. McKinly helped me after work hours with some of the keys so we could get them done faster, he glued some new key tops on due to router four damaging the key top notches and some keys came loose. I had to do some more gluing today. I did wood filling and touched up notches. We both cleaned the keys. McKinly also doubled checked everything and helped with some of the last few touch ups. They are done.

Steinway 107209: The outside, plate, soundboard, and between the tuning pegs have been cleaned.   Samick KJKJG0100: The outside, plate, soundboard, and between the tuning pegs have been cleaned.   Yamaha G3 1813811: The outside, plate, soundboard, and between the tuning pegs have been cleaned.   Francis Bacon 241008: Finished boring out the hammer shanks from the butts. Finished bolstering the hammer butts. Finished replacing catcher leather. Begun re-pinning hammer butts. Still needs bridle straps, felt replacement on spring rail and hammer rest, work on the let-off rail, and damper rod lubrication.
Warranty and Gallery items completed. Yamaha GH1: action backrail completed and rail slot has been cut. I'm going to guess it's going to be ready for deliver by Friday next week.

End o' the Week Notes: Bush & Lane 28077: Done Areli Dennis keyboard: currently filing keys, should be done monday. Kimball grand 132618: haven't started, should be getting started by tuesday. Kimball 357748: re-bushed about 5 keys

Knabe 128965: Aligned hammers (Bass Section is not finished) and finished whippens, leveled and eased keys (some may still be tight), Removed 2 broken screws from the key bed, and replace the broken wood. Tightened the strings to have better resonance.

Merrill 38180: Monday-Wednesday: working on action. Removed hammers, repinned, cut shanks and bored hammer butts. Removed and replaced bridle straps. About 2/3 of jack flanges reglued. Thursday Friday: out of town
Steinway 54630 Completed gluing rib separation, glued wedges in soundboard cracks, applied special filler, and sanded to 80 grit. Some action prep.

Colby 30776. Removed plate put all screws and hinges up for replating. Cleaned sound board as best I could without bringing outside. Bolts to secure front wheels are missing from before I started. Began work on action, took off dampers. Soda blasted and began rehammering.

Tunings: 9 pianos. The Bush and Lane will be tuned Saturday. Knabe 48335 Cleaning: Due to sickness on two days and participation in a move today, I completed little on this, however, the buffing is complete(with exception of the drill press buffing) --this was expidited after learning the proper usage of the wire buffing wheel. Moving: 1 move. Daniel's foot got spiked, but the sacrifice resulted in a successful and unscarred delivery.
Finished stringing Ya)maha G5. Have just a little bit of touchup to do on dampers on Farrand. Started getting the Gennosenschaft parts together.

Gulbransen: I've all the extra equipment and I'm onto the assembly of the piano, although I have some doubt since the piano was given up on and a few were disabled I'm curious if the old wiring of the tubing was correct or not, I also had to do a tini backtrack due to some of the old measurements for the wind motor weren't right and made the system run in a clunky way. I have to 3D print some of the parts that go on the bottom of the decks and to restore a key that was also disabled from the player
Broadwood 62561 Keys Should be ready to go back in the piano Baumeister 13233  Keys just need Better Final file Kholer & Campbell 189843 Keys In progress

Steinway 54630: I removed the music stand to change the key felt the felt of the keys and remove the front top cover to add felt to the holes to hold the piece steady. Whitney 86701: 
I started working on the action, took it out and cleaned it with baking soda. Removed rest rail and let off rail and the damper felts with steam. Removed the hammer butts, I bolstered each one and changed the catcher leather. I moved them to verify that they were firm and removed the bridle straps after that I bored them and put new bridle straps, tightened each screw and now I am regluing the jack flanges. I think I should be done on Wednesday midday at the latest.
Finished hanging hammers on the K&B.  All other QCs/repairs/etc. are done.

Steinway 64233 - keys squared and spaced. Hammers straitened. Just about everything else I did will have to be fixed or redone, I will get started with that on Monday. Kroeger 41368 - Dampers installed. Hammers are being prepped at station 10

Tunings =  3 out of 7, Acrosonic 784967: finished refurb (I couldn't get the buzzing out of some of the unichord bass notes), Werner 27854: intensive cleaning, polished practice rail and glued in new felt, new name board felt, I got all the black off the back of piano (spraying a quick shot of lacquer on it would look awesome),
#72873 Chickering - Continued redoing problem dampers.Been a bit of a frustrating process, but I'm looking forward to the payoff when I finish!

Pulled lid, hardware, music desk, tuning pins, unstrung piano, removed all cloth, made maps, buffed all brass except for lid hinge, we have about 10 more screws that need to be polished, and got things ready for plate removal.

Gulbransen 191466: aligned, spaced and leveled dampers. Glued bass damper felts. 

Strich & Zeidler 4261: started working on this today at 12:30 pm. Shimmed the keyslip, was gonna start leveling keys when I realized the balance rail needed to be lowered. Removed balance rail, routed the slots, reinstalled keybed and it’s workable now. Put all the keys back in. Will continue with leveling keys on Monday. With my schedule I expect I’ll need to work on it the entirety of next week. 

Tunings rescheduled: 2/4

Ludwig & Co.: I sodablasted the action, regulated all the butt hammers and started gluing the jacks.
Backshop: I helped put together the acrosonic.
 I trained the refinishing team a bit to take apart pianos and label them (I'm trying to get them to label everything the same as the technicians).
I repaired and glued the Emerson bench, it was very loose.

Photos: Wurlitzer 1293074 (before) // Heintzman 27802 (before) // Zwicki 10149 (before) // Duoart 96128 (before) // Everett 248443 (after) // Everett 34956 (before) // Kawai F081603 (after) // Baldwin 402277 (before) // Story & Clark 900975 (after) // Yamaha 272632 (after) // Yamaha T137829 (after) // Wurlitzer 983993 (before) // Kimball 143998 (after)

Worked on Steinway 55038. Finished installing the rest of the new pieces of the action. I began PRSB work for the Weber 33045 piano. I glued and secured all the separating rib points and am allowing it 24hrs to dry. While it was drying I reset the plate so that I could test the downward suspension. However the plate was not sitting flat so I began adjusting the main rest screws so that it sits flat. I removed the plate and finished my adjustments to the plate rest screws. I removed the screws in the ribs and replaced them with hammer shims. I began soundboard crack repair. I also asked Matthew to help me. I finished opening the cracks preparing the piano to be put under pressure so that I can add the shims. Matthew said not to put the piano under pressure yet. He taught me how to shape shims and dry fit them. I did so and finished dry fitting the shims and put the piano on blocks and installed the rod to prepare the sound board to be put under pressure pressure. I mostly finished taping the back of the soundboard to catch any stray glue. I put the piano under pressure and glued the wedges. After sufficient time I cut off the tops of the wedges and sanded them down flush. I applied epoxy to remaining gaps

The Andrew Kohler has been stripped, cleaned, and sanded. I have also taken some time into sharpening chizels and trying out new ways to give the chizels the burr that Julio likes but doesn't take so much off of the chizel. This has met with a mixture of success and minor not so success. Sometimes the burr is not even and tends to leave minor scratches, but these can be easily sanded out. I have tried the chizels with the extra burr on the painted white piano upstairs and it seems to take it off just fine but there may be some trouble spots. When it comes time to do this piano I suggest not chemically stripping it. Jacob noticed a machine that could remove paint more easily without use of chemicals (I think) and may be worth looking into. It costs about 70 bucks but the cost of stripper was about the same. On another note, here is the link for the bahco scrapers that I mentioned earlier. We could use maybe 3 or 4 of these. There are specialized sets but I think just some of these plain ones would suffice. 

https://www.amazon.com/Bahco-6-Inch-474-150-0-80-Cabinet-Scraper/dp/B0001P0PIG

Chickering 80147
This week I finished restringing 
When I got to the bass strings I noticed that the coil was resting on the wood of the bridge so through trial and error I was able to bring the bridge down  low enough that none of the coils would ever rest on it.
After failing a few times, and breaking a bass string by tightening and loosening, I may have gone a bit overboard on how much wood I was chiseling off.
I applied liqueur, and let it dry before doing a second coat.
I also noticed every right string on the groups of three, the coil was so low on the string that it was touching the pins.
Per Victoria,  I did not string these, and sent her dimensions to order replacements.
I then moved on to prep.
The first thing I did was go through all the hardware, and pull out anything that would need to be replated.
I went into the spreadsheet that says if it should be copper or silver replating, but the  cell for that piano was blank.
I already took the bag upstairs,  so whenever I am told what version of replating you want I will finish filling out the form.
Finally,  I started in on prepping the keybed. 
Next week I will finish that, and start action prep.

Continued DHRT on Mathushek 8409.  Finished adjusting damper rods with proper alignment an glued on new dampers. After putting on new dampers I found that the last 20 or so dampers did not have enough surface area on the wood part to properly dampen the sound. I worked on this for some time with Curtis. We both decided that I would have to remove those glued on dampers and cut shims that would increase the surface area on the wooden part of the damper. After steaming off the old dampers and cutting the shims I glued them to the dampers and held them together with rubber bands. Then I removed the rubber bands and cut new damper felts and glued those making more adjustments and repairs to the action. Many wippens were damaged and needed to be repaired and glued or have a spring replaced. After confirming proper dampening I began work on hammer heads. I bored the hammers and numbered them. Chickering 80147-
Wow it's almost two steps forward one step back with this piano.
I finished the keybed and got all of the felts on it looking nice. I ran out of key felt circles, so I put the keybed upstairs and I then started action prep.
When it was time to remove the spring rail, I couldn't find it.
It looks like this piano uses strings on the hammer butts rather than a spring rail.
There is actually 2 springs and 2 strings on the hammer, but adaline said not to worry about the second one.
She said it didn't do much and the  replacement hammer butts would not have the slot needed for the second string anyway.
Speaking of, We had to special order the hammer butts because of their configuration, so I moved onto saving all of the brass hammer butt flanges
Given that all of the hammer butt flanges are brass, I needed to unpin and replace the string on each one.
The bushing for that pin is in very poor shape. I protected what I could, but more than half of these bushings disintegrateed as soon as the pin was out.
I used up all of the re bushing felt and am about halfway done.
The new felt has been ordered.
It was the end of the day and I thought I could knock out the button rail, but after I got all of the old let off buttons removed, I found that we did not have the correct size for the let off screw.
I spoke about it to adaline,  and that part has been ordered.
I then moved on to breaking the jack flanges.
The glue on this piano is basically non existent.  Several of the jack flanges blew off when I was soda blasting, so I expected this to take no time at all.
Unfortunately I was Incorrect in my assumption. 
The wood on this is so brittle and fragile,  I spent all day friday only doing one section wood repairs around the jack flange.
Particularly the sticker. On several of these I noticed where the sticker had been pinned has cracked even before I even messed with the flange.
I guess chickering wood is known to be fragile,  but I still wonder if the action had been dropped.
Adaline said it may be prudent to send the action out to get all of the wood parts replaced. That perhaps that was why the action prep hadn't been done yet in the first place.Let me know if you feel that is the prudent course of action.

Kawai KG-6C 799457: I removed the old bushings and touched up the sides of the sharps. I vacuumed the dust and a dead fly off of the key bed. I tested the different bushing sizes to check what would work best for the keys on the key bed. I re-bushed the keys. Mason Hamblin 21816 Upright Cleaning and Prep I disassembled piano parts and labeled hardware: shimmed for new casters and installed casters: serviced trapwork, reconditioned wood, buffed metal parts including pedals, replaced felt, and reassembled; removed keybed and felt punchings, soda blasted, reconditioned wood, dressed key pins with scotch brite, replaced punchings and felt: Measured and recorded string specs and action bolt height, removed base strings coiled and zip tied, labeled and deposited in proper place to send off, removed treble strings; removed pin block pins: removed and labeled plate; sanded and polished plate screws, pressure bars and screws to finished grit: Action Prep completed, removed and labeled hammers (keeping 1st and last of each set) replaced hammer butts, reglued jacks in saddle, checked alignment, replaced some felt and repaired 4 small copper springs (unique design on jacks), replaced felt on spring rail, replaced buttons and felt on let off rail, scotch brite metal parts of and cleaned hammer rest rail and applied new felt, attached bridal straps and lubricated felt @ spring locations, capstan felt and backcheck felt. Placed parts in storage in organized fashion, labeled front and back the piano and keys boxes. Today (friday) I sanded to 80 grit the sounding board, located rib separation in 24 places, pre-drilled for screws at separation, took 3 or 4 at a time and using popsical sticks squeezed glue between ribs and sounding board, removed sticks and secured each screw with a small washer and felt punch to protect the sounding board, tightened until flush and cleaned the back of the sounding board. My goals for next week are to remove the screws at each rib separation location, drill dowel holes and glue and install dowels, complete wedge routing for multiple cracks, fit and glue wedges, sand flush and use wood filler where needed, sand to finish grit, and TSP back of sounding board. I also have to replace the cracked base bridge. I talked with Curtis and he suggested using epoxy at some pin locations on the high treble. My goal is to complete this next week. I may need an assignment for thursday/friday. I feel I have reached a point where I am ready to move on to the next level of my training. (I'm out of the thicker diameter angled metal tips for syringes and gluing of rib separation. I need these badly as well as a v-groove bit that matches the wood wedges. I have mentioned this before. The previous v-groove purchase does not fit the shim. It leaves gaps on top- I'll have to use the old bit again)

Mason and Hamblin 21816 -Removed the screws at each rib separation location (24), drilled dowel holes and glued and installed dowels. Completed wedge routing for cracks (20), fit and glued wedges, sanded flush and used wood filler, sanded to finish grit, and TSP back of sounding board. Replaced the cracked base bridge with new bridge cap, set downbearing height, drilled for pin holes, chamfered edges to match holes, sanded, set pins, dag, and filed tops of pins. Used epoxy at pin locations (approximately 2 dozen) at the high treble and mid and low treble bridge locations where there were cracks near the holes and/or pins were loose.  Conway 78344 - set the downbearing on the base bridge with plate in place, then removed the plate. Drilled holes for pins, chamfered, sanded, set pin and filed tops of pins. Baldwin 39464 -  prepped and sanded (320 grit) the exposed wood on the pin block behind the top cast string frame, cut pieces of veneer from places that don't show to cover and glue veneer in chipped spots that do show. The original veneered piece was beveled and to remove or install the plate it had to slip in or out from below and shift from left to right, but couldn't because of interference. We had to pull the plate and I routed the bevel straight. Anyway, we placed the main plate in the piano again (after routing 1/16" off the edge lengthwise) in the proper place and I screwed and bolted the plate to the sounding board- then tightened screws in both main and top plates. I am ready on Monday to cut the felt and begin stringing. The veneer was a problem; I thought it would be given more attention in the finish room and coated with laquer there, but one coat was applied from a can in the shop? For this coming week, I understand restringing should take about 12 hours. I don't know all that is involved, but give me something else to begin or learn and do  or reinforce this coming week.

Yamaha 3200604
This week I routed the rest of the cracks, and put wooden shims into the soundboard. 
I let the glue dry, then cut them flush and sanded the entire sound board.  I put epoxy into all of the low spots, and then sanded again.
I then moved onto action prep. 
I removed the damper felts making sure that the felt configuration had been documented,  and then moved on to the action stack .
I am Currently testing the hammers by doing a swing test.  Most of my hammers are only swinging 3 times rather than the reccomended 7 so I am having to repin 90%of the hammers. I wonder if this is because the felt is still newer and tight.
I'm about halfway done with this.

M: sick. Tu: lots of repinning on the Kranich and Bach 37121. W: Finished repinning, put action in to do regulation and other checks. The original parts are very fragile and a lot of them broke with any regulation. Some of the dampers have been tricky to place because when they dampen they click on neighbors. I think I have it mostly/all out, but I wanted to do some final checks after it was tuned and a case part was repaired. Dip was super low and aftertouch was super not there, fixing that made the piano feel much better. I also trimmed and regulated the practice pedal as best I could. Overall, the piano is about as good as I think these parts reasonably can be, and too much more finagling will lead to more broken parts. I still want to do some voicing now that it's tuned and listen for misc noises with the case parts on, but other than that I think it's as done as it can be. Th & F: more bridge work, I made the Wurlitzer bridge work with Plate Shenanigans™ so it's ready for lacquer. The Kimball is almost ready for template punching etc. I'll layer up cardboard on the soundboard while I chisel, that way it won't need to be re-lacquered. I will use a q-tip to lacquer the new bridge cap (with your permission) and then it'll be ready for restringing. Let me know if there's any priority pianos left on my list.
I worked on the action board a lot of the week, with traveling of the hammer shanks and removing felt. I am now beginning the process of the PRSB

Andrew Kohler 78279
This week I was assigned t je Andrew Kohler piano. I spent the week disassembling the piano. As I was disassembling, I noticed that there was a large amount of rust on many of the screws. I polished the ones found in the trap work. I was thinking about buffing every screw and replacing those that are extremely rusty, but i decided that most of the screws were not visible and could be left alone.
I moved on to trapwork prep.  I saw that the wood had some water damage, so i cleaned everything out and sanded the wood so that all the water lines were gone. I replaced the felts, and buffed the springs so there was no more rust.
I then moved on to pulling strings.
I filled out all the paperwork and am currently in the process of unstringing.

Coinola 175443: I got the keys flush, I put them through routers 3 and 4, I used the sanding belt as well to get the key top close enough that get flush would be faster, I removed old bushings and put new ones in. I also cut new sharps to length and corrected their lengths. I also touched up some of the sharp key sticks and cleaned up and straightened out the new sharp key tops (the band saw was cutting them crooked.) I replaced key tops on keys 45 and 30. 45‘s notch  was to low and 30‘s wasn’t on straight some of the wood was sticking out the side and the key had been made flush on the  other side so I replaced it. I also had to replace other key tops due to router four chipping them and loosened up one of them, and some of the key tops were not making full contact with the front of the key stick. So I had to Reglue those even though they were rubber banded. I started to touch up the notches and clean the sides of the keys. I need probably 1-2 more days. I’m hoping to have them done on Monday. I have my math placement exam on May 10th at 9 am. Would it be okay if I took here at work again? It’s easier for me to just go up stairs take it and then come back down to get back 
to work. McKinly and I are doing a half day on May 3rd, we are going to Vernal for a race that we have that weekend. Thanks Brigham.

Steinway 355193
This week I mostly finished the steinway. 
I figured it may be helpful if I relayed what I was spending the most time on in this project.
As far as I can recall this project only had 3 major slowdowns.
-castors
Getting the castors to fit on this piano was difficult, I made the holes too wide but not deep enough.
I was given advice so I think I can do them quicker next time, but this time it took a afternoon.
-screw extraction  
I had 2 broken screws on this project, one came out just fine, and one that took me about 3 hours before I finally gave up, drilled out the hole, and filled it with epoxy. 
Finally hammer butts.
Each section takes me a average of 2 hours with 4 sections it took me all day- I'm getting faster, 
but this is only my 3rd time doing it.
As of writing this, I didn't get a chance to start in on the Rudolf, but I have a few more hours left in the day so hopefully ill get to it.

Story and Clark: finished all pre reg, almost completed first pass I only need to do dip and then I'll be on to the second pass and cleaning. Should be done next week. Also I had tweak some factors with spring strength and rep lever height. There have been hammers that have appropriate spring strength coming from the checked position, but aren't strong enough to hold the hammer knuckle from resting on the jack while in a rested position. I've found if I manipulate the curve of the spring it can balance it out. Also good to note I've tended towards stronger spring strength in those keys rather than having inaccurate rep lever height.

Rudolf 22795
Continuing my thought from last week I figured it would be good to show you about how long it takes me to do each step. This is all napkin math and the times aren't quite exact but this is a good ballpark estimate.
Initial dissassembly 5 hours
This includes prepwork disassembly and breaks to help others
Castors 5.5 hours
This includes extracting broken screws chiseling holes to be bigger, and using shims to balence the castors.
String measureing 7 hours
This included measuring the strings, pulling them, and pulling all of the pins out
Plate pulling 5 hours this included mapping out all the screws, pulling them as well as the plate, punching out all the wooden pin circles, cleaning the piano with tsp, and cleaning my area
Bottom board 4 hours
This included cleaning and sanding the wooden and metal parts
What i have left-
Keybed cleaning and felt replacement 
Action prep

Rudolf 22795- Done! 
Keybed  2.5 hours
this included replacing all the felts and sanding the pins with scorchbrite.
Action prep  315
This included dissassembly, soda blasting, removing damper felts and getting everything  ready to be worked on
Hammerbutt testand repin 3 hours
This included doing a swing test for every hammer butt, and repinning any that swung less than 2 times, ir more than 7.
Hammerbutts install and travel 8 hours 
This included removing the old hammer butts, installing the new ones, and traveling so that everything was nice and straight 
Breaking the flange 7 hours this included breaking and reglueing the flange.
It would have been quicker, but a few of the stickers were not in good condition and it took about a hour to get replacements.
Final steps 5.5 hours
This included replacing the rail felts, replacing the buttons on the button rails, lubrication and cleaning up.
This took a bit longer because I ended up having to manually glue the button felts on. I am off Friday and  depending on some family stuff, i may take the Monday holiday  off as well.

This week I completed my first hammer reshaping after watching the video and receiving many helpful pointers from fellow techs. Mathushek 8409
This week I worked on this.
I got it all disassembled and the plate pulled. I am Currently working on getting the castors good. The old ones were very  recessed and in order to get them to spin freely I would have had to route into the soundboard.  I asked adaline for advice, and was told to shim it out so the piano is sitting higher.
It took a few tries to get the correct size, and when I did I found that the wooden shim took up most of the length of the metal castor hole. I didn't think that was enough support so I extended it by drilling a wooden plug from a 2x4 and screwing it in.
After I am done with castors, I will move on to action prep. Victoria said that this action was very weird and there will be a few non-standard steps to get the prep done correctly.  I'm excited for the new information,  but learning something new may be a little time consuming.

Mathushek 8409:
Monday: I used wallpaper remover and a razor blade to remove the cloth under the hammer butts. There is still a bit of residue that can cause issues, but hopefully, nothing that travel paper won't solve. If it is too much for travel paper, I'm going to have to either use steam or try to use sand paper to sand all the cloths  all away without sanding the wood down. 
Tuesday-wed
I removed all the felts from the old dampers, normally I would only remove the top felt only, but all of the felts were bad including where the damper springs go, so as per Victoria's instructions  i removed everything.
I was also told to remove the felts off the back checks. I spent a while trying to use wallpaper remover and a razorblade, but It seemed to be taking up too much time, so I used steam.
I then replaced all of those felts/cloths. For a few, I had to cut custom shapes out of cloth so that they would fit well.
Thursday
I finished replacing all of the felts on the back checls. While I was at it, I broke the old glue off the flanges and added new glue.
I also was given the replacement 3d printed stickers,so I had to add all the cloths that wwereneeded for that.
Friday.
I started putting everything back together.  I used the pre springed hammer butts because this piano did not have a spring rail.
I used the tap method to determine if repinning was needed, but none looked like they needed it.
Perhaps I was doing it wrong? It is odd to me that none in the box needed anything. 
I installed and traveled 2 sections of hammer butts. There leftover glue is causing some issues when traveling, but I think they look ok.
What I have left:
Traveling the hammer butts in the third section
Adding the flanges back on the third section
Lubrication,
Installing the rest rail cloth .
Shouldn't take more than a morning, unless that last traveling section is a particular pain in the butt.

Shaw 15614: This is the second key set that I have started this week. I’m currently working on getting keys flush. I soda-blasted. Also the sharps are in really good condition so I’m thinking that we can just keep the originals on. 

Also important note: I have 53 more days until I leave the shop. I still need to type up the document for the key part of the BLP manual. I‘m planning on bringing treats for my last day here. I have to admit, it makes me sad to be leaving. I love this team and when I think about how I don’t have much time left at the shop, it breaks my heart. But, I know that this isn’t goodbye. I will definitely still come and visit and come to the Christmas party with McKinly. But, I also just wanted to say thank you for giving me this opportunity and for giving me a chance. Thanks for allowing to grow and learn. Thanks for being so kind and patient with me. I feel like I have grown a lot since I have been here. This team has become a family to me. Don’t worry, I‘ll still bring treats and I will make sure that the treasure chests are made before I leave. Also I look forward to training the next key master. I love teaching and watching people grow, learn, flourish, and succeed:) I want what is best for this team and so I will prepare the new person as much as possible.

Friday My day was split between steinways. Steinway 423387 the buttons came in so i installed them. There were a few let off screws that broke in the action, but i was able to drill them out and fill the hole in woth epoxy. I'll let the glue dry over the weekend
Then i finished getting all the new cloth /felts ready to go. 
Take a straight edge. Mark where the center of the bottom screw will go, do that three times once on each side and once in the middle
Draw line using straight edge  and again with top screw.
Mark the center of each spring, and drill a hole using the 105 bit.
Cut plastic to size and make a jig. Using a 107 drill bit drill 1 hole into the spring rail. Do this for all. Then move the jig and do it again.

Training Christopher: The Gulbransen key set that Christopher is working on has been re-bushed, however, the balance rail bushings are loose and so the keys are wiggling more than they should. That one is on me. What I think happened was when the key frame on the key bed didn’t have its screws put into the right holes and so the key frame was crooked, which I didn’t notice at the time when we were checking which bushings are good, so it felt like the size we chose was the right one. But, after the key bed frame was correctly screwed on (thanks to McKinly fixing it after we noticed something was wrong and he also helped us to figure out what was wrong.) it was discovered that the balance rail bushings were the wrong size. I was able to get good tension consistently until I reached a pin with a tension of 0. McKinley suggested a CA glue fix but I'm not at all confident in that producing enough torque to bring that pin from 0 to the acceptable range, so I have left that hole and its pair marked with masking tape (star is on the problem hole). I did put in the next pin pair to make sure it was a problem with the single hole and that pair has good tension. - Note: This was my last normal week for my internship, and I have 54 hours remaining. Work on the Sherlock-Manning: This has been a long and frustrating endeavor. After trying different strippers and techniques we have gotten to the point where we were able to get off the a big amount of the primer paint out of the smaller crevices. This has revealed a lot of different dings, scratches, and damage to the veneer. Some of the veneer was sanded through when trying to get out the primer but a lot of it was there previously. The stripper that worked the most efficiently was the Aircraft Paint Stripper (aerosol) or the stripper that we have to put in a spray bottle. (Note: That stripper is very strong but also tends to jam up the spray the bottle. If I use this stripper---or the aerosol---again it would be nice to have a completely vacated room as the chemicals are harmful to those not wearing the proper safety gear and the back spray may land on other pianos in the room and cause damage to other finishes.) There are specific filters and masks that are used for chemical sprays that differ from masks that filter out sawdust or anyother airborne organic material. I think it would be wise to purchase the proper filters for when paints are being sprayed in Julio's shop. Some of those might be old and in need of replacement. If we plan on doing more chemical stripping, we will need a couple more spray bottles that would only be used for the stripper. For corners and details it would be nice to have specific tools and/or brushes. Again, I think this is only if we plan on doing more chemical stripping in the future.

This has had varied success. Occasioanly the chisel will bite down in the wood in spots. Bryan has suggested using a specific tool that he has access to; a wood scraper or planer. He says this is easier to handle, leaves a smoother finish, and is not at risk at leaving gouges in the wood. He will bring some on Monday for us to try out. There are different variations of this for different tasks. (i.e. ones for curved, intricate work)

If we want an actual workshop course, we would need people to the official one in Denver, in January or July. I think they hope to do more specific workshops in the future closer by but I don't know when this is going to happen. I really think that I could get chemically stripping to a faster speed and would still like to try other processes.

Weekly Report in Julio's Shop

We've figured out how to more appropriately sharpen the scrapers. Instead of using a whetstone to take off old birs I've used a 600 grit sand paper and that has been much more effective. I've asked Jacob to order more scrapers. It would be nice to have different ones with different angled burnishes. Some angles are good for taking off old finish but other angles are good for taking off light shavings of wood. On the Adam Schaff piaon we tried a different filler on a corner that was heavily damaged but It didn't turn out too well. We will let it sit but I don't know if it will turn out. Julio will teach McKinley and I on placing veneer soon. I wonder if taking off some old veneer and putting on new veneer in some places would be quicker on the heavily damaged veneer instead of adding filler and sanding. On a side note, one of the most difficult things to sand is inside corners where two parts meet on the piano. If we can take it apart we do but in the case of where it is glue it is difficult and takes a lot of time and effort. I don't know if there is a certain tool or way to do this easier but the power sander cant reach it and I would be hesitant to use any of the pnuematic devices we have.

I've finished regulation, am back to damper work on the Steinway. I'm looking forward to finishing it, it feels close. This week was mostly spent on painting the soundboard. If I were to do it again, I'd probably start off differently than we did, but I think Julio gets the general idea/method. He asked me to work with him again today to help him put on that veneer on the Lester piano for that curved portion on the front. The drill press is fixed, the middle pulley (a variable pulley) had siezed on the shaft from rust/oxidation. I cleaned the pulleys, re-aligned the pulleys, spent some time cleaning/lubricating the areas of the shaft I can access without some additional tools/dissassembly, and some fine tuning. The press does still have a tendency to have that pulley sieze when put to the highest speed, which it works fine at high speeds but fails to properly readjust to slower speeds. I haven't screwed on the top, it's just placed on--it will function fine that way and the screws are embedded in the plastic so they won't be lost. I wanted to leave it easily accessible so that if it siezes again, it can be easily accessed and corrected. It may take some time for the lubricant to brake away some of that exess oxidation on the shaft, I'll try to make a point to periodically open it up, re-lube, clean exess & check how it's doing.

I'm almost through with polishing, I have only the pedals and pedal shafts left. I'll be here an hour or two after closing today, should be done with that and moved on to the next items in the list--I'll do a quick update before heading out the door. For the Steinway, I finished damper work except for that one flange I showed to you the other day. The damper flange didn't completely glue: it comes undone after I remove the wire I've used as a make-shift clamp. It is still attached (a spring that is pressed through both pieces of wood), but it's quite floppy. If you'd like me to take it out and get it fully repaired, I can do so--I expect since all the travelling and bends have been put in place, the damperwork would just be re-setting timing and alignment to strings, I think I could do it in about 2-3 hours. I'll wait to hear back from you on it, I can focus on the chickering first to get it done by the dealine, and come back to the steinway for that last repair if you like.Either way, I'll wait to hear back on what you think.

Finished tuning all 5 pianos! On the Baldwin however, I did not get too much further on. I sanded that area on the plate and punched out the tuning pin bushings, pinned all the flanges on the whippens and glued half of the heels on the whippens as well. I think that was about it. I also finished the quick regulations on all 4 of those pianos you assigned me in the gallery. I think some of them are still having some issues though, more particularly with double hitting, even though there seems to be plenty of after touch after lessening the blow distance and re-adjusting the lost motion.

Microfiber cloths have been purchased. I got the gallery floor cleaned. I got the pianos dusted and wiped down. The conservatory enterance has been dusted, and the plants have been dusted as well. I got the Casio O fixed, hopefully for the last time. All of the benches have been assembled. I got Brigham's office dusted and vacuumed. Dish soap has been ordered for the break room. If l can have someone with me, it will probably only take an hour, maybe an 1 1/2 hours at most. I am need of some more longer screws (about 2 1/2 or 3 inches long).
player still in progress! I'll write some more in a bit, but for scheduling purposes, please put me down for another 12 hours and I'll be done.
I am now done with the Hobart M Cable. I am well under way with the Howard 81084. I will need between two to three days to complete this piano and I will be ready for another project.

I am almost done with the action prep. I still need to polish the damper lift rod and the other rod next to it. The spring rail felt looks pretty good so I was wondering if that needs to be replaced or not. I also need to put new casters on the piano. I should be able to finish up on Monday.

Finished glueing the heels onto the whippens, and installed tge whippens onto the action. Half way done replacing, spacing, squaring the new back checks. Got startes on removing the felts on the dampers. I also did the music stand repair and caster. And the field cleaning went well. I will obviously need a whole ton more time to finish.

This was an okay week.

For the first time ever, a leg failed on us. There was no way to know that the leg was weak. Nor was there any indication that the leg would break. But lo and behold, as piano movers thus lifted the piano, the leg broke off at the joint, and the piano came crashing down a shocking 12 inches. The earth moaned and quaked and the room was filled with silent screams of piano movers. There was gnashing of keys, and wailing of strings. But we took it back and got it fixed, so that's nice. Lyre was also damaged and that had to be taken back, but that has been fixed and delivered. 

Micah and I now have trust issues with legs. They are the fuel for our nightmares. 

Other than that, everything has run smoothly. Did a dump run today. That was fun. The lady at the entrance was almost nice to me.

I have just been working on the bench room. I have one wall completely finished, and the other wall mostly finished. I could really use some help getting the rest of it done. My hands aren't big enough to hold the level and the plastic strip at the same time, and if l could have someone to come help, it would only take a few minutes

The Hailun 178 player install is complete. Just started the 198 yesterday and its going even faster than the last. It definitely helps to have everything close by. I’m unsure how many hours I will need still, but I need to start working on the next one on Friday. My cutoff for this project is Thursday the 13th.

The Baldwin/Howard spinet pinao is now complete! I am currently regulating the Baldwin and could possibly be done with this piano by the end of Monday and might do a final inspection Tuesday morning. I will then be ready for a new project.

end of the week notez: Chickering 18229: done. Kimball 42155: done. replaced a couple bushings that fell out in the process. Apollo 28765: in progress. salvaged good ivory. mill off tops/fronts today. Best guess is finish up with filing friday as well as working on whatever's next while things dry during Apollo work. Also, helped Jake with a handful of keys he was polishing. Sanded out some deep gouges on keytops before polishing on the baldwin 182143. Also, soda blaster had hose and part break, spent some time getting a new hose for it, and salvaging some parts from the other soda blaster. I got all those new bushings cauls, we can start up on that C7 again whenever you wanna schedule it in again.

I. Tunings. 5 completed and logged. So many beautiful notes. II. Assist Julio. Sanding of Chickering 182229. An upright learned the meaning of sanding as well, and a Baldwin Grand plate got cleaned and rust scraped off. III. Miscellaneous Moves. A lot of happy people got their pianos moved and set up. I met at least 7 new people because of moving pianos!

Will begin hammer drilling first thing sturday morning. Treble dampers look great.

Regulation & Cleaning: I'll have the piano ready by next Friday's day end. AWESOME! I can't wait to see and hear this piano completed!

Finished the back checks. Finished removing felts on the dampeds. Replaced all felt on key frame and the felt on the end of the keys for the dampers. Replaced all let off buttons. Put on new shanks. Bored new hammers. Tuned all but the Steinway, which we already talked about.

More paper towels have been ordered. All the Casios have been taken upstairs. I got the 160 set up. The Casio area looks nice again. I got the vestibule floor mopped. Player on the 198 has been installed and the Keyscan is also installed. Just need to finish that mute rail and then any troubleshooting. The Baldwin is done, and the Hallet Davis is also complete. I also did everything else on my calendar. The Winter is currently in progress. There are some key top that are chipped off on the lips. I will keep you updated daily I'm so glad there won't be jury duty!!!
Apollo 28765: done. Emerson 102313: in progress, old keys and sharps removed, currently gluing new sharps

The Autotone, Baldwin 182143, and Samick are in their final stages. I think Julio will be able to paint the Autotone before he leaves. The Baldwin 182143 and Samick just need to be satinized. I know what to do to finish that if you’d like me to do that on Monday.

I basically still have all of regulation to do on this piano. I'll be here late tonight, and tomorrow for most of the day to try and get this done before deliveries on Monday. Bridge repair is done, I have yet to drill out the bridge pin holes and restring that small section. There are two additional strings missing in the treble I'll be working on tonight. The leg has been completely repaired: the new plate was thicker than the old one, I had to route out an additional depth, I've plugged the holes, re-drilled them, fit the plates, etc. I've also plugged the holes from the previous repair of lag-bolts through the leg and into the piano, there is a small remainder of wood missing from the lag bolt heads digging into the wood. I've cleaned up the hole with a forstner bit, I think I have a plug cutter large enough I can completely fill the hole, and refinish that section without marring Julio's work if you would like that done (or I can show you what I would do before doing it)--otherwise, I'll let it be and move on. The cheeck block stripped hole has been plugged, re-drilled. Cleaning is done other than a wipedown of the soundboard underneath the strings. I'm looking to be done with string repairs, seating coils, and any other repairs and through setting dip on regulation before heading home tonight.

Finished those 4 tunings this week! As well as the quick two jacks fix and the quick action tightening and bridal strap repair as well as a couple other repairs. On the Baldwin, I finished rebuilding the trapwork and lyre. They are now beautiful! Half way done hanging hammers, installed agraffes except for a couple that need that washer thing underneath so it can be tightened at the right spot.

Swiffer dusters have been ordered, in addition to big black garbage bags. l got the cleaning finished. Bench room has been finished as much as it can be. The Samick is nearly complete. I just need to touch up on damper regulation and I will be done. The Winter is currently on pause due to white keys being replaced. I got as far as I can on that piano. Emerson 102313: done. keys in piano. Winter 323395: in progress, currently filing keys, 'bout 1/3 done Starr 76199: where'd this come from? sneaky sneaky. All tunings complete and on time. All Christmas Eve satinizations are complete. Hailun 121 Walnut 210552 satinization, roughly 55% complete. My estimate is that it will be complete by 2:00ish on the 26th of December. MERRY CHRISTMAS!!!!!!!!!!

I've finished all string related work (fixing coils on tuning pins, becket bends, etc). I've finished traveling hammers, anticipate finishing bedding keyframe and starting on key height tonight before leaving the shop. I've just got regulation left and a tuning & the piano is ready.

Delivered Pianos. Sushied. (That was good stuff, thanks again) Slept in. Christmased. New Yeared. Chocolated too much probably.

The Baldwin is done, and Sterling is done. One note on the Baldwin: two keys are chipped. I do have an appointment with a Neurologist Tuesday morning. I don't know if I will be in that day. If all goes well, I will come in for a half of day. Here are the Italy vacation days: February 9th-20th

Done with all QCs, tunings, repairs, and Apollo bass strings.  161 needs one more day because of the 5P.

out of town

Captain's log: Stardate 96747.25. reports of my demise have been greatly exaggerated. You will be happy to know, Admiral, that the USS 8p was finished on time and is ready for delivery. We have also incapacitated the enemy Romulan ship, the Yamaha G7, and it is now free floating in the dark vacuum of space with a hole in the middle of it! Scans show there are no remaining life forms on board and we have begun retrofitting it to be serviceable for Starfleet. By our estimates, the construction should be complete by Thursday. first, let me talk about what still needs to be done. on the 4 pianos that you assigned me,  the g2 still needs a good regulation. all of them need some fine tuning on the regulation and the c3 is the only one that didn't get tuned. both Hailun grands feel great and are in tune. also switched out the bushings in the pedal on both of them. The Cable is cuurently in progress. It will probably be most of next week if not all of next week to continue on this piano.

Finished the Knabe and started the Mathushek on Tuesday. I will need all of next week to finish the cleaning and action prep. The right leg will need to be reinforced somehow. Well, I had a funny “bit” that i wrote the last time. I’ll spare you all the puns and just get to it. We had what seemed like an unusual amount of warranty work this week. Most of it was VERY easy stuff to fix. As far as things in the gallery, I ended up taking three days on that HALLET DAVIS PLAYER, readjusting the pedals, doing a complete regulation, lubrication, adding screws to address the key slip from binding, easing the key bushings, etc. I have to say I’m still not really happy with it. I’m kind of frustrated with how it turned out. It checks all the boxes of regulation, but it doesn’t feel right to me. All the plate screws are off, polished, and in the cardboard map in the box. One of the plate screws was super tight and took several of us working together to find out howe to get it out. Also, I tried routing out the wheel wells to fit the rubber casters but it still hits the sides when theres nothing much left to take away (I'll upload a picture to the album). Then I just need to clean the back once the piano can stand back up. I started the action prep portion today and will need at least 4 full days to do that.

Mathushek: I still need less than a day for the Mathushek action. I need to redrill one more hole for the regulation screw and then put it in the action.  Knabe grand Cleaning: All done except for polishing the the lyre rods and pedals so that should only take 20 minutes or so. Knabe 48335: I started aligning the damper heads to the strings so I didn't get the dampers glued on yet. so I'll need a day or a day and a half to do that.
Everett: hammers are installed.  Probably 3 days for regulation.  All tunings and QCs are done.

The Starr 76199 regulation is almost done. I will only need a few hours to finish regulation. NOTE: Not all of the Baldwin Acrosonic hardware are solid brass.
