We are running low on certain front rail and balance rail paper punchings stock # 338-003, 005, 007, 010, & 012. #333-007, 010, & 012

Knabe Grand cleaning is finished. HD & Hailun polisings are finished. Finished the Mathushek action today. For the Knabe dampers and hammers, I finished all the dampers and just have to line them up better. I started a worksheet for the hammers and just have to measure some more angles and I will be able to start boring them on Monday.I might need till thursday to get the hammers bored and installed.

Brinkerhoff - finished.  All tunings/QCs - finished.  Matt Johnson's bridge - finished (in about 12 hours).  I have mixed feelings about sharpening the chisel on the buffing wheel.  It de-flattens the chisel after a while.

SHOWROOM MISC: Finished strings and worked on easing the balance rail on the 151. Wouldn’t mind a couple more hours, but it’s not entirely necessary . KNABE: made good progress this week and will be able to finish up in a few hours (5 hours).
Mathushek restringing is in progress. With the pins I have left, an estimated time will probably be another two to three more days until more pins arrive.

By the end of this week I got all the hammer heads bored and installed the high treble section hammers. I'll probably need two days to get the rest of the hammers installed (once the shanks arrive) and straightened out.

The Baldwin needs to have the pedals installed and checked.  Everything else should be about ready.  I ran into a couple of repairs on the Everett, but it's close.  The regulation is finished except for the dampers.  The damper lift needs to be set (and the pedals, which aren't installed yet).  Probably just one more day, not counting trapwork/assembly/tuning.

Samick: String installed, fixed pedals Yamaha U144989: Pedals Regulated Winter: Tuned D3, sanded backs of the damper levers and also had to regulate damper timing because the hanger was corrected. Installed felt under pedal to limit movement. HD 152: Tuned. Placed pitman and lever back, however, it needs some work with regulation. I’m planning on finishing this today, but I’ll update this before the end of the day (Friday) Knabe: Didn’t work on the Knabe this week. I’m still confident it only needs a day’s work.
The Andrew Kohler is currently in progress. I am projected to finish the Andrew Kohler on time next week. As scheduled, I can move on to the Knabe.

Knabe: I just installed the rest of the bass hammers this morning and will need at least half a couple of hours making them look as good as possible. Steinhauer: on the cleaning side, I just need to clean up the trapwork, and install new casters. I should just need an hour to finish that and then I can move to the action prep.
The Everett still needs some damper work.  I think the damper rod might be bent.  The Baldwin is close, just needs one more day to finish the regulation.

The Knabe regulation is going slower than I expected. I had to repin a lot of the whippens that were loose and that took me all day today. I am about two days behind. Andrew Kohler notes: Everything is done on that except for buffing the casters. It doesn't seem to do much on the casters themselves and it is steel so it is not going to match the rest of the hardware.

The Steinhauer cleaning is just about finished! I just need to clean up the rest rail, attach the bridal straps, make a couple flange repairs, and then I’ll be done. I just need about an hour to finish everything up.

Fischer: done.  Starr: 1 more day.  Conway bass bridge: done.  Baldwin L: done.  Kimball: tenor dampers are ringing quite a bit.  I don't know if it's at an acceptable level or not.  Otherwise, it's ready.  Everett: time got cut short for sales training, but it's probably good enough for now.  Maybe another hour or two before it goes out.

Steinway: Regulation is complete, however there are some last things that need to worked on before it’s all done. There’s a let off screw head that broke that needs to be extracted and replaced. A0 backcheck is loose in the hole and needs to be epoxied. The first damper on the treble side needs to lift more, it seems to be choking that note. The damper upstop rail hasn’t been regulated. The Lyre is still not attached and pedals needs to be regulated. There are two or three hammers that are rubbing against their neighbor and probably need to be squared/spaced or traveled. I was able to do three pitch raises this week on this piano, so I’m guessing it’s probably close enough that it can be tuned soon. Anyway, I would estimate another 5 hours at the most to fix all those problems and get the lyre on.

The Knabe regulation is done! This is one of the harder pianos that I have regulated so far because there were repairs that came up during regulation. I moved on to the Mathushek restringing and that will take most of the week to do. I am going to come in for a half a day on Monday and take the second half of the day off due to the holiday.

Kimball 108839: I will be able to start working on the action by Tuesday as I'm almost done cleaning the piano. I just need to finish the trapwork, clean the plate and sound board, and polish the bolts and screws. I should be able to do all that in under a day and a half.

Baldwin is done (thanks for fixing the buzz).  Starr is done.  178 is done and ready for Texas.  Stacey put a QC on Friday that I didn't have time to finish.  It's being rented on Tuesday.  It looks like it would need about 2 hours.

Bramhall Steinway: Work was finished. However, Julio did notice that the middle pedal is not working the way it should. I must have rushed that when i put it on last minute. Bush & Lane: Had some problems that Bryce and I worked on, but it seemed like it was ready to go. Hailun 10786: Installed MIDI strip. Today I did notice that the controll box had come unglued from the piano mounts. Just need 20 minutes or so for me to epoxy them back on and reattach them to the piano. Laffargue Grand: Tuned and regulated pedals. There are a few things that I wrote in the notes on the calendar. Take a look. Knabe 172366: Cleaned and did some easing on the keys. Looking at the action i would say i do need to reshape the hammers, but since they are fairly new it shouldn‘t take too long.

The Mathushek restringing is complete! I have also done everything else on the calendar. I started the Everett cleaning and still the action prepe to do. It will probably be most of not all week to do that.
Steinhauer: the bridge is complete and just needs to be glued in. Kimball: I started the action prep yesterday so I’ll probably need about three days to finish that!

QCs finished for Starr, HD 160, Knabe grand, and Wurlitzer.  Everett is finished.  Steger dampers are done (except for some bass dampers, which we ran out of), and I've started prepping the hammers.  Probably more than a week's worth left.

Laffargue: DONE. Winter: Added lost motion, although it is very wide compared to what we normally do. Just the consequence of pulley keys, the type of felt at the end of the keys and the fact that it’s a spinet.  Voicing is still bright. Did some needling and didn’t get a lasting result. The crown appears to have been lacquered at some point, so we might have to use something to move it away from that crown.  Music desk doesn’t have anything to prop it up, beside the top lid of the piano. Check it out.  Casters are too large. I don’t think we need to change them, but it’s probably a good idea to point it out to who ever did the casters. The pedals on most pianos are anywhere from 2 to 2.5 inches from the bottom of the pedal to the floor, but this one is about 3.5! Again, not a deal breaker, but it’s kinda weird. Lol. Hailun 10786: DONE. Knabe 48335: It appears to be screws for the mute rail and also an orphaned mute rail with stripped screws in the back. Otherwise, it’s ready to go. Steinway 267959: Worked on dampers again on Thursday. Also tried to fix the thumping of the backs of the keys hitting the damper lever by steaming, then needling then adding profelt to the felts. Timing is much improved, but probably could use another hour to address the middle pedal.
The cleaning and action prep is done. I am on time as far as scheduling is concerned but I am a little ahead because I had some time to install the flat dampers

Kimball: Finished the action prep for this. Mathushek: Damper heads are tight and aligned to strings. Steinhauer: Bass bridge was glued in I started restringing and have done strings 88-72. I’ll probably need all of next week to finish the restringing.
Steger dampers are finished.  Hammers are prepped, and the strike point is set.  Probably need about 3 days to glue them in plus any squaring/spacing.  Then on to regulation.

The Player install is coming along quite well, however 20 hours was definitely very ambitious. Still need to finish the pedal solenoid, add the main solenoid cover, wire management, and all software calibration procedures. I would predict another 10 hours to finish.

The Lexington action prep is currently in progress and will need another day or two. Everett : I had to hit the pause button while the bass bridge was being worked on. I don't know if you want me to install the bass strings and then continue on that piano. I know you mentioned that I was on the list to restringing the Kimball. I will watch my calendar.
Steinhauer: I finished putting on all the strings and just have to weave in the string cloth and use a coil tightener on all the pins. I should be able to get that done on Monday.

All QCs are done.  Steger hammers are installed, repinned, squared, and spaced.  I also eased all the keys.  Should be able to finish this piano this week, depending on how much troubleshooting there is.

QRS Install: Still need one more day on this one because of the lost time on Monday. Everything is installed, i just need to do the software and troubleshooting. Steinway: DONE Showroom items: DONE Knabe Grand: Quick Regulation is done. Did a pitch raise and started tuning, but it still needs to be finished up. Maybe another hour.

Everett Notes: hammer installation in progress. I am also having damper clearance issue on the bass section. the bottom of the damper is interferring with the wires next to it. I might have to go with the other kind of dampers to give clearance.

Steinhauer: Got the dampers installed, hammers preped and bored, hammers glue into shanks, and have the middle and high treble hammers glue into their butts. I'll propbably just need 2-3 hours to finish the bass section and fix the spacing in the other sections.
Platt is finished.  The Steger is done (except for trapwork/assembly etc.).  The 33P is well underway.  Should only need 3 more days.
My report is pretty easy this week. The Player install was done on Monday and just needed to be tuned.
The Everett is now complete. I still can't find a suitable action knob that will work. The restringing on the Kimball is in progress. That will take me most of the week.
I finished the hammers and spaced them. And did half the regulation. I just have to do the back checks and spoons and then I’ll be done with regulation! I’m assuming I can finish that by Tuesday!

Hailun 33P: There are some big cracks in the polyester on the music desk behind the front hindged piece.  Also, both sides of the case have come a bit loose near the top. Kimball: cleaning done. hammers reshaped,  and leveled keys. I would say probably 3 more days to finish regulation, tuning and QC.

The Kimball restringing is done, and the Horugal CA glue treatment is also done. The Emerson cleaning and action prep is in progress. It's probably going to take most to all of next week to thrououghly do everything. I will be a little late on Monday due to a dentist appointment at 9am

Steinhuaer: is on hold as we wait for the pedals. Just need to do the trap work on that one.  Lexington: I got most of the dampers on (need more trichord dampers but I heard we’ve got some on the way?) shanks have been glued into hammer heads. I’ll most likely finish the hammers by Tuesday and then get the regulation and trapwork done by the following Monday.
Estey: Bridges are all glued on.  Treble bridge still needs holes drilled, notching, and bridge pins.  Probably 2 more days.

Kimball: Finished refurbishing. Still needed a tuning, but was pretty close. Emerson 47337 cleaning and action prep complete with exception to casters (waiting for single rubber casters to arrive). I can now move on to the Estey restringing. I cannot find the the little rod that goes below the capo bar in the slot. We might have to get a new one.

Steinhuaer: is all done and assembled. Lexington: Just finished the last set of hammers and just need to go through and straighten the bass section so that shouldn't take more than an hour. QCs were done.  Estey bridges are ready, but I just realized I forgot to do the casters.  The M&H hammer flanges got repinned, several cracked birds eyes repaired, and old hammers removed.  Should be able to start prepping hammers on Monday.

MIDI Strips: Done! Added some extension cords to all three pianos and i think that will help with the experience for just a few dollars more. Showroom Misc.: All items have been finished. MCKinley showed me the new improved damper timing and I approve. Should be ready to go! Kimball: I am mostly finished with cleaning, although I suspect that the trapwork and whatever parts that are missing might need some help once i get to that point. Estey restringing is done! You already told me what to do next so I will go ahead and do the dampers and hammers on the Estey. I need to take the 24th off because I will be in Cedar City that day. I can get regulation and trapwork done by the following Monday.
Estey casters and QCs are done.  The Mason & Hamlin hammers were prepped and mostly installed, just another hour or so to finish.

the Kimball piano is tuned and ready to go. at least within the parameters that I was given. it definitely needs a little bit of help with the bushings on the keys, and of course refinishing, but then it'll be ready to make lots of money for us! $$$$$$$$$$$ Haha!!! Our evil scheme is working PERRRRFECTLY!!!!
The Estey is in progress. Still need to install hammers and do regulation. Probably a couple more weeks to do it.

One more day on the M&H (some regulation, new string, tuning).

Schiller is coming along. Did a bunch of cleaning, much more than I did with the last one. I think you probably need to make a decision on the pedals being replated or not. Anyway, give me another week on this one.

The Estey is almost done. I still have backchecks to align and here are a few things that I want to fix so give me Monday and depending on how things go, I might be ready to move on to another piano on Tuesday.

M&H and QCs: done.  C&C: Bridges are off.  Ribs are all reglued and holes are plugged.  I couldn't find the soundboard shims and track so I moved on to the bridges.  I made templates for the bass bridges, removed the caps, and plugged the pin holes.

K&C KJLEG0203: Finished within the 3 days. There are a few issues that i noticed though. Mainly the timing on the dampers and the damper pedal probably need to be looked at, but otherwise it’s good to go. I’m excited about how this one is going to turn out, especially if we’re refinishing it.

The Acrosonic is currently on pause the keys will be redone. I will need to work on something else until the time comes to continue on the Acrosonic. I have also done the Everett 248170 on Friday (today).

Bench for Kawai is satinized and is under piano. Kimball: brought strings up to tension, gave it a pitch raise the next day, and I’m almost done with regulation. I need another day on it. I saw that I will be working on the Lexington next week and I’ll be able to finish that by Thursday.

Soundboard is all shimmed and planed down.  I assume I'll be sanding it as well?  The bridge caps are well underway.  I still need to make the template for the long bridge.  I have a couple ideas on how to do it, but if you have a suggestion let me know.  I'm not sure how much time I need next week.  The long bridge is a bit of a wild card.

Schiller: didn’t get a ton of time to work on this piano, but you already know this because you’re in charge of the schedule. :D  I was able to work on polishing and putting the trapwork back together. Now, before you ask, yes had 25 hours. But I do want to point out that I only had 4 tunings this week, which is a downward trend going on for a long time. Let’s get that “reasonable effort” back up.

The Schimmel is now complete! The Everett is also almost done too. The upper treble bridge does have cracks and will need to be replaced. I don't think I would be comfortable doing the treble bridge on that Everett yet, however I would like to learn how to do a bass bridge in the future. We need to buy more baking soda. I used the last of them.

Kimball: I completed it Wednesday morning. Lexington: I started on dip today and just have to align backchecks and finish the trapwork. I should be able to do that by Tuesday morning. I’m aiming to have it done on Monday though.

218 is ready to go.  It didn't need much.  C&C: All the caps are glued on.  Upper bass bridge is done except for pins, and the lower bass bridge is right behind it. Schiller: I'm pretty much done, but I still need to connect pedals, pitch raise and do final assembly. 2-3 hours depending on if you want me to tune it as well.
Lester 23209 is done! Conner and I started on the cleaning and action prep work for the Clemons and Crane. It will probably be three to four more days on it.
Finished the Lexington, polished the Hallet Davis top, cleaned the Steinway, and on the Winter I should just need three more days.
C&C notching is halfway done.  2 more days to finish and install.

Schiller: Done and ready for tuning. I ended up doing a CA treatment on Monday because the pins were just a tad too loose for comfort. Samick: Didn’t get time to work on this one this week.

The Clemmons and Crane cleaning and action prep is done! I started on the Lester spinet. Probably most of the week to do the refurbish. I need to take Monday off for Labor Day, I will be in Heber Valley that day.
Winter: Finished the winter today. Do the lid hinges need to be polished for a refurb? If so, that still needs to be done. Started the Currier today and I'll need 4 1/2 or 5 days to finish that.

C&C is done (except for some finish from Julio).  The Lester bridge is well underway.  How much repairing do you want me to do on the soundboard?  Just gluing ribs, or shimming cracks as well? Please do both the ribs and shim the cracks. Thanks!
Samick: Medium rare Done. Hamilton: Extra Well Done

Acrosonic 412667: Finally done! Lester 210271: Done, except for regulating dip and a string replacement. We are out of the piano wire size I want for that replacement (19 1/2). Lester bass bridge is done.  The grooves are all routed in the soundboard.  Probably 2 more days to glue them in and clean everything up.
SANDING, SANDING, SANDING! KEEP THEM DOGGIES SANDING! RAW HIDE!
A lot of sanding!
Sanded a lot and did some moves. Looks like my report from last week was deleted but I’m almost done with the Currier. I just hace regulation to do so maybe 2 days on that.

We experimented a bit with the chemical strippers.  Julio’s not convinced yet, but I think if we got a good system down it could save a whole lot of time.  The issues with it are mostly logistical.  I don’t think it would be too difficult to figure out.

Clemmons and Crane: Stalled on the restringing for the time being. Waiting for the new pins to come in. Kimball upstairs: cleaning done. Reshaped hammers. Still only had one day to work on it, so still need a couple more hours at least.
The Schimmel and Nelson restringing is done! The Lester is done too and was able to do the string repair. I am ready to move on to the next piano.
Just about finished the Currier. I just need to regulate the backchecks which shouldn't take more than an hour and then I can start the Astin Weight.
The Lester is done - ready for Julio to finish the soundboard.  With the QCs, tunings, and warranties I have next week, I’ll need three days to finish the Everett bridge.

Kimball 351416: Pretty much done. Just needs a tuning and assembly. An hour and a half to finish this one please. Hailun 178 226300: Pitch raised, tuned, lubricated and replaced pedal bushing. Schafer & Sons: Didn’t get to this one this week. I still need it downstairs, i guess. Winter 323395: Tuned and fixed jack springs that had popped out. Hailun 218 19866: Tuned and fixed rattle on the front beam. Adjusted pedals, and cleaned off from the construction dust. Still needs you to look at the damper timing on this. It’s a bit early. It was off the scale on most of them that i tested with our torque wrench.  Clemmons & Crane: Didn’t get to this one this week. Really looking forward to finishing this one up!
Irving 67267: Cleaning and action prep is done! I started the Lester today, prepping it for restringing.

Finished the Currier and moved on to the Astin Weight. Astin Weight: I finished the cleaning, rebuilding of trapwork, and cleaned/polished the keys. I just have to do regulation, lube the action, and reshape the hammers. I might need till the end of Wednesday to get that done.
All QCs, tunings, and other gallery work is done.  The Everett bridge got pushed by a last minute QC/tuning.  Needs one more day.
I have more details on my calendar which has been up to date all week. Kimball: DONE Clemmons & Crane: didn’t get a chance to do any stringing.
Lester 23209: Restringing done! Everett 5618: Restringing getting close to being done. I will need about half of Monday to finish.

Astin Weight: is finished. Schimmel & Nelson: I started installing the dampers today. Everett bridge is complete and installed.  All tunings/preps/QCs/other gallery work is complete except for the Helman, which I didn't get to.  The Lester dampers are partially done.  I'll need one more day to finish them.
And it came to pass that there was a pluque set upon the land and it did consume the entire week. And lo, save Monday, all was lost.

Sherman and Clay 1749: The cleaning and action prep is done with exception with exception of the new hammer butts which have not arrived yet and a minor action fix which will take only a few minutes to do. I will be ready for the next project.
Schimmel & Nelson: finished hammer installation today. Still need to finish dampers (when they arrive) and will need till Thursday to finish regulation and trapwork.

7P: tuned and buzz fixed.  Helman: tuned and C5 repaired.  K&C: tuned and QC done.  Mathushek: tuned (twice), voiced, QC done.  It’s not perfect, but I think it’s as good as I can get it.  Lester: dampers are nearly done, hammers have been bored.  More than a week left.

All QCs were finished. The Clemens and Crane stringing is progressing along, despite me almost messing the understring felt. Jake actually came up and suggested that I add that before I got too far. Saved my bacon for sure! Then of course like a moron, I got in a rhythm and didn’t remember to put it on after the break in the treble. (Eye roll) Anyway, it’s all good now. Lesson learned. I’m coming in on Saturday to work on it some more. Hopefully get this pumped out. I would say, plan on maybe Monday still on the C&C and then moving on after that.

The Baldwin refurbish is done and everything else on the calendar is done. The trapwork on the Hailun 151 is almost done. Locating the screw holes will be the hardest part for me. I will be ready for new piano to work on.

Schimmel & Nelson: all I need to do is damper regulation. All gallery work and in-home regulation is done.  The Lester dampers are finished and hammer hanging has started.  Probably about a week left on this piano.
All elbows replaced on Mueller and Haines.  90% done reshaping hammers.

Everything on the calendar is done. I am ready to move on to another piano. Thanks!
Finished the Schimmel & Nelson earlier in the week. Emerson: I am just under halfway done restringing so I think I could finish by Wednesday.

All QCs and tunings are done.  I got a late start on the Kimball on Friday, it needs about two more hours to finish the tuning and fix the extra action noise.  The Schaeffer needs another couple hours to finish up the sharps.

Continued working on the Hospe: The plate is off and is getting refinished; the key bed is clean and punchings have been replaced; all the plate screws and bolts have been sanded and buffed. I used compressed air to clean most of the dust off first. I replaced the felt strips on the rails and started to remove the damper heads, and will start steaming them next week.

So this week I worked on that kimball upstairs and honestly I still kind of hate how it feels. Rough regulation. Also made the damper timing a lot later, but it still feels very heavy. Stacey said that the customers especially were conserned with the first octave and a half being too heavy. Unfortunately, I wasn't able to make it "feel" much lighter. It's a lot better regulation wise, but I really don't enjoy playing that piano.
Everything that is on the calendar is done, including the pianos you scheduled on Monday the 3rd. I am ready to move on to a another piano
Emerson: I finished restringing on Thursday and the dampers today and moved on to the hammers. I think I would need a full week to finish it all.

Kimball is done.  Schaeffer is done.  All QCs are done.  The Yamaha leg repair is handed off to Julio for finishing.  I'll find a second to glue them in early next week.  Didn't get to the Lester this week.

I fitted them to the cracks and glued all of them, so now I just have to use the planer to level them all next week. Then I should be able to put the plate back on.
This week was a crazy one! A lot of different things demanding my attention. Anyways, just slap the clemens and Crane in there all week. Let's git 'er done!
The Erard is still a work in progress. It is a lot of work! I haven't even started on the action and trying to finish the damper system. It will be another week to spend on it.
Emerson: Still working on the regulation. I will need at least till Wednesday to finish regulation and trapwork.

Still working on the Hospe 136670. this week Jake showed me how to plane the shims that we put into place last week. I sanded them down and gave the piano to Julio to put lacquer on. I need to blacken the top, notch the holes and bisect them, then install the bridge pins, sand them down, and glue it all back on the apron.

What an awesome week! So many appointments! Seriously, you need to teach me your trick of how you are able to schedule appointments at 6 am! Anyway, got a lot done on the Clemens and crane this week. Next up is the bass dampers and then hammers!
The Sherman Clay bridge is now done! I went back to the Erard to work on the damper system as well as the action.
Emerson 47337: Finished on time (Thursday). Irving 67267: finished dampers and started gluing shanks into hammer heads. I will need a full week plus a day to finish hammers, regulation, and trapwork.

I installed the capo bar and then shadowed Jake while he showed me the process of restringing. I will start restringing the Bass strings next week.
Sohmer and Co. - Hammers reshaped, action lubricated/tightened, action back in piano, naturals leveled/squared/spaced. - starting to level/square/space flats

BEST. WEEK. EVER. Ok, that might be slightly exaggerated, but only a bit. Got the Treble and Midsection done.

Sherman Clay: Restringing is in progress. The goal is to be done by Wednesday before Thanksgiving which I could be done by then. The Erard action is slowly coming along too. I am not coming in to work on Friday due to Thanksgiving.
Irving: I am now working on regulation and will need till Wednesday to finish the piano.

I finished re-stringing the treble strings on the Hospe 136670 and made sure the strings were spaced according to the story stick made previously. I should be done re-stringing completely by next Wednesday.

So, I wanted to say thank you for all the appointments I’ve been getting lately! It’s been really busy and will definitely help with Christmas. I also am very grateful for the shop work: Clemens & Crane I finished gluing hammers and now they need to be hung.

Sherman Clay: Restringing done! More string braid needs to be ordered. We are also running low on full sets of tuning pins and will need to be ordered for future restringings. All of the Yamahas in the showroom are also done. Ready for another piano unless you want me to still work on the Erard.

The Lester is midway through key leveling.  All other shop work is done.

I should be done gluing and fixing the hammers by next Friday. Then I will start on cleaning the key tops and re-doing them with help from Brigham.

The Erard is coming along. The repinning and the knuckles have been bolstered, which did not take long than I thought, which is nice. Yamaha 2134607: Finished at the beginning of the week. Irving 67269: Finished yesterday. Took 4 hours and 17 minutes. I think the ideal would be around 2 hours, because of wire management. I think you could slap it all together in an hour, but I like it to look good and be confident that it's going to stay good for years to come.

Deena Winters action: Bushings are trimmed and they are great. The keys don't move side to side like they did before. I also wiped down the keys with soap water and a rag to get all the residue dirt off.  Munoa: All the keys with chips in the ivory have been repaired. I also wiped down the keys with some soap water and a rag to get all the grime and dirt off. I also took some scotch brite to the sides of the keys to get rid of the finger oil that has accumulated over time.   Steger 26388: I was able to route the tails and sides of the keys as well as the corners where the black sharps fit. I messed up one key (87) so I have to replace that key top with a new one. I started the final filing on the first few keys but I ran out of time.  Key ends need key tops. I tried to use those new key tops that you left in the key room but the color was off. Not only that, but the difference in height from the other keys and the separate key top weren't even. I even asked Stacey to tell if the colors were off and she said they were. If she notices, the costumer will notice as well. I'm going to wait for the new key ends to get in and glue those on.   I just have the first and final filing, gluing the key ends and shaping those and replacing that key top (87) and the keys will be done. It will take me 1 solid day to finish all that, maybe less.

Krakauer 32610: I replaced all the key tops and am on the steaming process. I was able to steam keys 1-51 and caul them. I still have to steam out the bushings for the rest of the keys. I can then go back with keys 52-88 after the glue has had time to dry, trim those bushings and do the final filing. It should take me about 2 incomplete days to accomplish.

Strohber 127312: sanded all of the trapwork components, finished laying out where the components go except for the player parts. (I'm not sure if i should transfer the player components. will you leave instructions about this?)

Lakeside 29891 - The temporary legs are installed, looks like it will work really well. Gluing the top of the pinblock went well. I'll glue and clamp a few spots on the edges as well. Didn't quite make it to gluing the ribs. I'll start that on Wednesday. There are many spots where it is separated.
Yamaha C7: Done! Acrosonic 317242: I am almost done with this piano. I only need a few hours on Monday to finish

This week I nearly finished the Kimball Grand 369956, I've found that there are some keys that are rubbing up against eachother, even though the plastic top is flush with the wood and I need to figure out why, aside from that they are done. I finished doing a final file on the Yamaha 103440, or rather went back and filed some of the heads so that they weren't clicking together when the keys were pressed. I had started on the Hardman Grand 92975 toward the end of my shift on Friday and only got the action off before shift end, so those keys will be begun on Monday.

Wurlitzer Grand: this week I soda blasted the action, installed the hammers, and I’m currently bolstering the knuckles and checking which flanges need repinning. I will probably need the next week to finish the hammers and do regulation.

Chickering 38350: finished restringing. Replaced broken agraffe and restrung those notes.Cleaned damper action, repaired a couple arms and installed. Install dampers and damper felt. Started making a plan for what to do with action.

Washburn 12464: this week I worked on the action. I replaced the buttons and eyescrews of the let off rail with new ones, I had to remove the felt that came with the new buttons because these were too big compared to the original ones and I didn't want there to be any problems with regulation afterwards because of that so I replaced the felt with thin button felt. I replaced the felt of the spring rail and the cloth. Installed new hammer butts that still need more traveling. I glued all the jacks. Glued some dampers (were the screw goes), repinned about 3 of them. A screw broke while I was tightening the hammer butts, it's already taken care of. I'll continue traveling and spacing the hammer butts on Monday.
Strohber 127312: finished sanding all trap work components. Finished putting on all the trap work components on new bottom board. Started putting a bevel on bottom board
Lakeside 29891 - The ribs are all glued to the soundboard. There were many places of separation all over. Sanding soundboard in progress. Also cleaned keybed and did some screw buffing.
Acrosonic 317242: Done! Samick IJKBG00127: Done! Yamaha G1 789564: Almost finished, I only need about four hours to finish.

This week I finished the Kimball Grand 369956, as well as the Hardman Grand 92075, and on Friday I pulled the keys from Bladwin Acrosonic 317242, and will start that one officially on Monday of next week.

Wurlitzer Grand: This week I finished bolstering the knuckles, repinned the flanges that needed repinning, I aligned the whippens to the hammer, refurbished the trapwork, and I'm starting damper regulation. I'll need maybe 4 more days to complete dampers and the regulation.

Started refurbishment on action on Chickering 38350. Took off all hammers, soda blasted knuckles, cut off shanks and re-drilled knuckles. Removed the butterfly springs. Drilled holes for new screws in whippen flanges. Removed whippens, soda blasted, removed old felts and created jig to drill wider holes. Started to build replacement whippens.

Washburn 12464: I finished working on the action, traveling the hammer butts, on Monday. Harvard 3319: I started working on it on Monday. Took off all the metal hardware and pedals, put them in a labeled bag and left them outside your office to get replated. I did the trapwork, cleaned the keybed, took off the bass strings (I left them too outside your office). Removed the rest of the strings and tuning pins. I removed all but one screw on the plate, it’s a tough one but I’ll figure it out on Monday.
Lakeside 29891 - Finished filling all the cracks. Sanded up to 400 grit. Glued more of the pinblock sides together that were cracking. Sanded pinblock. Next big thing to do is the bass bridge.

Yamaha G1 789564: Done! Conway 1846: Done! I have not seen any issues so far on this piano except for the tuning. Bluthner 52980: Currently in progress. I will need one more day to finish and will ready to move on to the next piano. All other items are also done.

Baldwin Acrosonic 317242, I replaced the tops and sharps and got the first filing done. I'll trim the bushings on Monday and do the final filing which should be done Tuesday at the latest. Steck 22531, I finished the final filing on Steck, the last thing those keys need is new bushings, I hope to have those done on Wednesday. Haddorf 91218, I steamed out most of the bushings on Friday, and I'll finish that on Monday and put in new bushings on Monday and those should be done by Tuesday.

The Wurlitzer is coming along. I’m still in the beginning stages of regulation and will need to work on that all next week. The Salt Lake Wurlitzer tuning and cleaning went well. The piano pretty in tune and was only a couple cents flat when I went to tune it. I also vacuumed it out and wiped it down. I’m almost done with Becky Tomlinsons piano regulation. I scheduled a time with her to come back at 1 on the 15th. I just need to do damper spoons and checking. I also need to tune it still,  address those repairs you put in the notes, and fix 2 broken hammer butt springs. The pedal spring is fixed now.
Chickering 38350: finished building replacement whippens. Started re-felting, bolstering and re-pinning all whippens. Started building replacement knuckles.

Harvard 3319: It’s done. Removed the plate, sanded and buffed all screws. Buffed, cleaned and polished the capo bar. Worked on the action prep: removed damper felt, soda blasted the action, replaced the cloth of the rest rail, the felt of the spring rail, replaced the buttons and eye screws of the let off rail (I had to replace the felt of the new buttons with thin felt because of the size difference with the old ones, just as I did with the Washburn), I installed new hammer butts and traveled them, and I lubricated with teflon powder and CLP, and I cleaned the metal bar behind the dampers. Installed new casters.   Washburn 12464: cleaned the metal bar behind the dampers and lubricated. While I was brushing the teflon powder on the dampers a few of their springs broke and others I noticed were not tense, so I replaced their flanges.    I was done with the Harvard by 10:30 today, so for the rest of the day I helped Julio with sanding.
Strohber 127312 Finished cutting a bevel on the bottom board. Shined strings and front leg casters. Started replacing casters.

Lakeside 29891 - the pinblock got stuck in the plate again and caused a little more damage that I had to repair. I think the repairs went well. I trimmed the edges of the plate yet again to help the plate fit in a little better. I am on the step of transferring holes from the template to the bridge. Mason and Hamlin 34240 - I started sanding the soundboard while waiting for glue to dry on the other piano. Checked for rib separation, and I did not find any separation. - The Lakeside still has cleaning and action prep to do later on.
Bluthner 52980: Done! Beckwith 138292: Done! Smith and Barnes 31620: Done! Geo Steck 22531: Hammers are currently in progress. I will need most of the week. I could be done by Thursday.

This week I finished the Baldwin Acrosonic 317242, as wel as the bushings for Haddorf 91218. I steamed the bushings on Steck 22531, will get those done by Tuesday, and I removed the tops and routed the tops for Washburn 12464, should have those finished by Wednesday.

Finished Becky Tomlinson’s regulation and tuning this week. Both QC’s finished. Tuning is going well. Still averaging just above 2 hours on a standard tuning. Had a couple rough pianos to tune this week. Wurlitzer: I think I just need one more day to complete the regulation! It’s been fun learning how to do it!

Chickering 38350: finished building replacement knuckles, butterfly springs and let-off buttons. Re-felted let-off buttons and action frame. Finished assembling hammer shanks and flanges including pinning with appropriate size. Installed all hammer shanks, butterfly springs and let-off buttons. Did some adjusting off hammer shanks. Will install whippens when screws arrive. Next up is jacks and back stops.

Hallet Davis 52505:  Took off all visible metal hardware and pedals and passed it on to get replated. I did the trapwork, replaced the casters, took the bass strings off, cleaned the plate, sanded and buffed it’s bolts and screws I could reach. I’m still working on the action, I already took the damper felt off, glued back on some bridal straps that came off, replaced the rest rail cloth, replaced the buttons felt of the let off rail, bolstered the hammer butts (with yarn), and replaced the catcher leather.  I was supposed to start working on another piano today but I’m a bit behind schedule, mainly because the trapwork and steaming off the damper felt took me much longer than expected. Sorry about that, I’ll be faster next time :)

Strohber 127312: Put on 2 more casters on the last hole of the caster I drilled it out and put in a plug, waiting for glue to dry. Vacuumed piano. Polished the action holder rods and knobs. Started polishing capo bar and tips of tuning pins.

Lakeside 29891 - finished the bass bridge. gave to Julio for laquer. will glue back in on Wednesday. Also worked on sanding key pins in key bed. Still needs to finish cleaning the back and action prep. Mason and Hamlin 34240 - did not work on this week.
Geo Steck 22531: Done! Astin Weight 8028: In progress. I still have a little ways to go on this piano. I might be done as early as Wednesday or no later than Thursday.

I finished putting in and trimming the bushings on Steck 22531, so that piano is finished. That took about 1.5 hours totalish between usings the bushing punch as well as inserting the key cauls, and trimming them after letting them dry overnight. Washburn 12464 was a stubborn one, I took about 37 hours on that one, and unfortunately I had to replace a majority of the whites because as I was using router number 4 to route the notches, the screw that secures the stop to get the notch lenght right, the wood that it screws into got stripped and I didn't realize it until I ended up routing a few of the notches too short and had to replace the keys. So now all I have left to do one those keys is the Bushings and Final Filing and they will be done. I was able to Soda Blast the keys for Hallet Davis 52505 before my shift was over, and so I got started on those keys at the end of the shift. I am getting better at making sure the keys are straight at the key tails, as well as getting the notch to a 90 degree angle, I'm seeing improvement, and I couldn't be more excited to be a part of this team. I hope to learn everything there is to know about working with such wonderful instruments, from regulation to voicing to tuning!

Tuning at the Salt Lake high school went well, sluggish dampers caused by a bad repair to the middle pedal system in the action cavity. Both the grand and upright action models in the window have been regulated. I haven’t touched the one we’ve been using for our trainings but I think we’ve regulated it during trainings. Wurlitzer regulation is finished! I fixed E3 where it was letting off too early. U3 QC also was completed on time. Washburn: just finished gluing ribs back to soundboard and I’m in the process of removing the old lacquer on the soundboard.

Hallet Davis 52505: Finnished. Repined all the hammer flanges, cut shanks, replaced bridal straps, replaced one eyescrew on the let off rail because it was broken (head was cut off), bored hammer butts, installed hammer butts and travelled them, lubricated with teflon powder and cleaned and lubricated the metal bar under the dampers. finished the cleaning and action prep on the Lakeside. Mason and Hamlin 34240 - sanding sound board in progress. Will need another project to work on during glue drying periods for the bridge.

Yamaha QC finished. Washburn: finished the soundboard and bridge work and handed off to Julio. Friday I put the plate on and got the right felts in place. I will be ready to start putting on the strings on Monday. I haven’t restrung in a while but three days might be enough to get it restrung.

Hardman 92975. Got all the ribs re-glued to the soundboard. Wedged the soundboard then completed sanding, installation of shims and epoxy to fill thin cracks. Installed dowels. Sanded. Hallet Davis 52505: Done! Wagoner Berlin: Done. The damper regulation is not perfect and been trying get rid of ringing and it is better but not perfect. I not going to work on Monday for the Memorial Day holiday.

I also need to use the planer on the bottom board and the screws for replating and pedals and lock assembly are in the box with the remaining hardware of which is all labeled and in separate bags according to assemblage. F: repinned hammers and whippens, touched up jacks that were off and all rep levers went from 0-2 to 6-8/9. did a traveling pass. Plan for monday is final travel, align hammers to strings, travel whippens, regulation. If I dont finish Monday I will Tuesday before lunch.

Needham 277547: This week, I cleaned finished cleaning the treble strings after removing the bass strings from hitch pins in order to remake the bass bridge cap. I bundled the bass strings together with zip ties and hung them off to the side of the piano. I had made new caster spacers, and routed out the caster mounting points so the caster would spin freely. NOTE ON SCALING: i dont know whether it was during manufacturing or if a tech was bad at replacing strings but the scaling was garbage. It went something like 13-13.5-14-13-14-13.5-15-17-20 with LOTS of outlier strings of crazy different diameters. At first I really ignored the outliers and marked the bottom sheet on the clip board in the piano but after i realized more so what was going on I just wrote my own scaling (i did a project on rescaling in college so i know a bit about it) then checked the piano for where scaling changes could happen and came up with the top sheet as my suggested scaling. The  I took off the treble strings and started removing pins. Also with S&S i did voicing and strike point stuff. it is ready for qc.

Conway Serial # 78344 Cut and drilled spacers for front casters and installed all casters. Removed bed and punchings- soda blasted, sanded wood, scotchbright key pins and bright boy, new punchings. Removed all wound strings in order and strung on a short piece of piano wire, zip tied, labeled and placed in container beneath Victoria's desk- no missing strings. Measured the treble strings with caliper and marked each section on a sheet. Loosened the tuning pins, capo and removed strings. Removed tuning pins. Made a map of bolt configuration-removed alll screws, measured shoulder height with caliper and removed hanging (frame) pins and knobs. Removed frame and tagged. Sanded from 80 to 2000 grit and buffed all mounting screws- etc, buffed capo. Wedged the sounding board to create a bow- used two step boring process for screws. Used popsical sticks to allow room to glue between ribs and soundingboard- then 3/4 " screws to tighten soundboard to ribs. While waiting for glue to dry (overnight) removed damper felt on action, soda blasted action, removed hammer rest- also felted the silencer bar for the player. Removed the 3/4" screws on sounding board next day. Drilled for hammer shank dowels- set and glued dowels. Sanded old finish off and sanded dowels flush with 80 grit- hand sanded all corners and hard to reach spots-80 grit. Using the small v-groove routed wedged groove- fit the shims, taped the back side and glued 3 lengths. Hand planned the shims and hand sanded to flush finish @ top surface.

Conway Serial # 78344 SOUNDBOARD: Routed V-grooves on remaining cracks in sounding board and fit wedges to match. There were 6 more on Monday if I remember correctly- 9 total V-grooves to route and matching wedges to glue. I described the gluing process last week. I finish sanded 100 to 400 grit the clean soundboard and filled imperfections and any miniscule gaps around V-groove with combination glue and sawdust from the sounding board. There were not many gaps. To do this I added one drop of water to a bit of glue in a plastic cup and brushed a slight film of glue over the gap. I blew the dust onto the glue and when dry, sanded with 150 grit. Will have to sand again to 320 grit if I can find 320. All I could find jumped from 220 to 400 grit. Hulio did not like the few minutes sanding without the vaccuum but I was leary to use sawdust from a different source and had not saved previous sawdust. Next time will keep sawdust from pervious sandings. Did not trust the dust in the collector. If this method is not satisfactory I can try other methods. BRIDGE CAPPING: I did not remove bridge from soundboard because there was no apron. Removed bridge pins with pliers. Made mylar template exact perimeter of bridge. Drilled two pilot holes to position mylar on each end and taped mylar to bridge. Marked hole positions with permanent marker trying to center marker point in the hole. Removed mylar. Planed .3125 Inch off bridge using a power planer (5-6 passes) to level. Cut bridge capping of solid maple material to approximately +.0625 inch oversized width and length with same matching end angles + .0625 ". The maple bridge cap measured .5625 inch thick leaving in all .25 inch proud on top (after gluing) to account for downbearing and needed adjustment after the frame is in. I felt 1/4 to 5/16 inch proud was too much but was following directions and will find out. Placed pin nails in pilot holes of bridge to position bridge cap, tapped and marked identical pilot holes on cap. Drilled two matching pilot holes on ends of bridge cap. Did some light sanding on bridge. Drilled two step process- 4 holes to match #8 X 1" screws in bridge and clear passage through bridge cap. Glued both surfaces of bridge and cap, placed cap through pilot pins and screwed by hand the cap tightly to the bridge using 4 small felt punchings to protect wood and keep the screws from damaging the top surface of the cap.Will do this next time to protect the sounding board when turning the screws. I will also use masking tape over the shank dowel holes to prevent wood fibers from lifting when drilling. Dowel holes were not crisp. Left to dry. Have yet to remove the 4 screws and 2 pin guides in order to flush trim using a straight flush bit and router @ the perimeter of the bridge cap and sand edges to remove router marks; also add possibe finish sanding touches to soundboard. ACTION PREP: Dampers have been removed from action using steam method and action was soda blasted- this was last week. Tuesday of this week, I removed the old hammers and stored them in a labeled box upstairs with one hammer slip-tied to the outside of the box. I kept the labeled hammers for each section separate in the piano box. I began installing hammer butts and then had to clean up. That is where I stand to this point. This week was two days for me. I rarely get sick, but thought it best to stay home the remainder of the week. I did let Victoria know via Lora forwarding my text. I hope I am well enough to come on Tuesday. I received direct deposit check for labor I think to date. Thank you.

M: showroom notes: I got most of the vibrations out of the Kanbe, I think the rest are pretty faint and either caused or amplified by the neighboring piano. The biggest fix was tightening the whippen screws by the dampers. YC: part of the sticky keys was the upstop rail, so i shimmed that and eased necessary keys. Most of the lost motion came from the left pedal regulation, rest was fixed with capstans. QRS just had the one piece of foam. White YC was shimmed, each sections "ideal" hammer line for after touch was slanted downwards towards the treble, so averaging means aftertouch is higher in the treble end of each section and lower in the bass end. I think it could be further refined with a pass on letoff and drop. Hailun damper guide rail was moved. Gerhard: continued hammer fixes and started squaring/spacing/string alignment. I think the hammers are starting to actually look quite good overall (here's hoping they actually are), although the strings aren't moving quite as much as i need them to to be perfect, and the bass section is impossible to not be a little spotty.  Tu: a lot of the strings couldnt move far enough to align with the middle section hammers, so I need to re-space them. Unfortunately that means a lot of the middle ones have rolled a lot and aren't level how they used to be. The bass section is also being bent a lot to align with the strings but isnt near as noticeable. W: hammers are done for now, i'll do a final pass at the end but im moving on to try and keep schedule and come back with fresh eyes to hopefully reduce correction time. did a few misc repairs on the action then started key leveling. Th: keys leveled, started regulation and made decent progress. i've been having a rough mental health week so if it gets better i'll try and do some work on saturday and hopefully finish up so i can start the keys monday. F: regulated dip to aftertouch, started backchecks, did some action tightening, LOTS of damper work. dampers are pretty much done, im touching up some ringing from dampers whos timing was affected by spoons, will do a final pass monday. i havent spaced backchecks or bridal wires yet or done trapwork. I will be done monday before end of day if trapwork stuff goes well and the GS agraffes go smoothly. The biggest time loss on this piano for me was the hammers and some miscommunications that I would say took up almost an entire day with instruction/video time. If I get to do another set of hammers they will go significantly faster, especially if the action isnt designed so all the hammers need to be bent. I will also do a final pass on hammer spacing, but it shouldnt take long.

Kohler & Campbell ILL 01622: finished the rest of the keys adjusting for aftertouch in the key dip using the .25 and .50 punchings, checked damper lift (1 was late and 3 were early so I bent the damper wires accordingly, then I regulated damper timing/ spoons (using the method that Jason showed us in the PTG meeting, and Adeline coached me on this also), I was eventually able to fine tune the timing extremely well. It was a this point that I missed some work going home early one day and staying home sick another day, I then adjusted the back checks left/right and for/aft, Victoria examined my work and pointed out some areas that needed more attention, I got to go over everything again (this time much more confidently. I was surprised at the effect that let-off the key dip had on the lost motion, and then when tightening up the lost motion, I then had to clean up aftertouch in the key dip again. I went over the back checks again, fixed a broken bridle strap, adjusted and lubricated the pedals, finally I checked the key bushings by depressing the sustain pedal and watching for "slow risers" and identified 25 keys that were having problems, then I started addressing the key easing.

The plugged pins were pretty loose but going up a size didn't help much so I did a CA treatment. Given how easily the plugs deformed I think any hole big enough to fit the pin would have been loose. The final drill bit size was .265 and the pins are .286, but a few minutes after ca treatment they could hold pitch and measured over 100. A final check on monday will confirm the effectiveness. George Steck 20893: this is an experimental project for me, there’s been a lot of figuring out things along the way and thinking through the best/most efficient ways to approach this while always keeping in mind the beauty aspect and goal. This is definitely an art project and I’m having a lot of fun, I feel like my knowledge in color and tone is coming into play a lot. So, first I planned out how many tails I need of each note, heads and fronts. I have all the fronts I’ll need, I found these among the reno things, they aren’t Ivory but I believe they’re made out of an Ivory imitación material so they have a specific color and visual texture effect. I selected tails from our salvage bin, not worrying too much about the color thinking I’d be able to clean most of the yellow out. I sorted divided them based on which key note they fit best, then I cleaned them (trying different things, baking soda+lemon juice wiped off with hydrogen peroxide worked best), but then realized that I should’ve cleaned them after choosing a color/tone have them all match because even though I cleaned them thoroughly I had to set aside a lot of the tails because they didn’t match the color I chose (they’re too yellowish). As of now, I have all of the A tails I need, except for A0, 1 B,  2 G, 3 F, 1E, 2 D and 2 C (I still have to find the right C8). So it’s going in the right direction, now that I have a sample key color to use as comparison I’ll be able to find the remaining tails I need. We have a lot of decent heads, but I still haven’t cleaned them and sorted them by color. The good thing is now I know how I like to clean everything and match, I have a plan.

M: read the book and some other PTG resources Jason helped me find. Learned a lot, took quite a few notes. Checked dampers and labeled ones that need some adjusting, found a method to get bushing cloth thin enough and started rebushing the FR. Tu: continued rebushing and looking in to leather for the hammers. Thinning normal bushing felt actually worked for the balance rail too, though for both some of the mortises needed to be trimmed with an exacto to fit properly. W: finished bushing, worked on escapement levers/repaired a broken one, started fitting keys to keyframe. Th: More key and escapment lever work. Had to take out some bushings and trim the mortises more. Built jig for letoff outside the piano and its fully adjustable. F: finished building letoff jig, fitting keys that needed new bushings yesterday, lubricating kapsels, took some measurements for letoff and key leveling, basically started regulation. Renner has key busing leather that I'd like to buy for the tops of the hammers for a few reasons: 1. It will be the proper width and stretch direction so will only need length cut as opposed to the leather we have. 2. It can be bought at 1 and 1.5 mm thickness, 1 mm being the desired thickness for the caps. I think two strips would ensure I have enough to cover all the hammers. If you feel this is an unnecessary purchase I can try to use the leather we have, though I think it will be pretty soft. The method there seems to be using lacquer on the crown as needed to voice. The piano is fairly soft in tone from what I played today, but not so much dim as much as powerfully quiet.

Mason & Hamlin 21461: finished removing keytops, made the veneer pieces for each key for cladding, cladded all the keys, trimmed down the excess veneer, glued new keytops. I used the #1498 German Keytops (“light cream”) because they’re slightly wider than the Hailun keytops that were too thin for the new size of the keys. I used the router to cut down the notch on key #1 according to the wooden surface under the plastic but it turned out to be way too much. I replaced that keytop with one of another set of German keytops, the problem I ran into here was that there were like 3 boxes labeled as “German keytops, light cream” and so I thought I would have plenty backup, but when I looked for the stock #1498 I noticed that the box I used was the only one with that number and the others are #1496, since these are a lighter cream (not entirely bright white) I came to the conclusion that the ones I used are “cream” and I mislabeled them a year and a half ago so I did this to myself. The difference isn’t very noticeable, you notice it when you know what to look for, and the keytops that I used look awesome (they’re not yellow) it’s all creamy but #1 is slightly more on the white side. On the more important and noticeable matter, the gaps are looking very nice and fine. I’m at filing the plastic down and notches stage, I’ve done 1 section up until now.

Knabe 163220 (71): I touched up the sharps with scotchbrite and the wood part of the sharp with the paint pen. I wood filled keys that needed it. I got the keys cleaned and buffed ( Mckinly helped with the last few.) I corrected key number 3‘s length due to it being longer than the others. McKinly glued down a key button that he noticed was coming up. He helped me get the keys eased. They are done. 

James Duckworth‘s Client’s Keys: I soda blasted, stamped the keys and then removed the old key tops. I started out routing some of them off, but switched back to the way I usually do it due to the keys having inconsistencies with how they were routed before they got to the shop. I would have kept on adjusting the router. So I thought it would be best to just manually remove them. I was worried about the plastic causing a fire.  And another reason why I manually removed them was to compare the thicknesses of the key stick to the new key tops. I also checked the key lengths and there was 5 keys that were shorter than the rest. I corrected those keys and I also corrected a key that was crooked on the front. I sorted the keys into three separate groups depending on what needed to be done to them. I started out with two groups originally, which were build up or route down. There are four keys that needed neither. So now I three groups. I used the new key top to compare all of the keys thicknesses to see what was routed to low or not low enough. I routed down 47 of the keys (one was from the build up group, so I could know where to build up to.) I built up the two keys that needed it. I put travel paper on key 88 and I cut out a wood piece for key 85 and a added a small piece of travel paper to it. I‘m now in the process of gluing on new key tops. I need probably 3-4 more days. For notes and more details about the keys, it’s on the Google doc.

started HD and Hailun, did warranty clicking. Tu: finished hailun and almost finished HD. W: finished HD, did string replacement, started work on Steinway C. Did a tuning, assessed dampers, did chucking treatment on all keys. Th: eased keys, rebushed damper guiderail, aligned backchecks, mainly the ones that were rubbing against their neighbor, re-bushed a FR that fell out. Hailun warranty was a twofold problem, first damper timing to pedal was incorrect, most ringing was from this and re-timing worked. Second, the trichord dampers needed to be heat treated and squished so they sat further down in the strings allowing for more contact from them and their flat pair. There is still some ringing in the very high treble damper range that isn't the dampers fault but could be minimized by weaving some felt through the non-speaking length of the midrange and treble at their next tuning. F: Steinway damper work, S&C qc, deckard capstan shim, bandsaw work. My idea for the steinway dampers is to flip them around, glue a shim on top and trim with the key router, then install a spring tension screw from an old set of whippens

Steinway “C” 9799: removed action to access keys, there were 12 ivory keytops that needed replacements.  I removed all the heads and looked for the best matches, I found most of them relatively fast but the A0 one was tough. After finally finding a decent match for that one I filled some of the key wood with filler to make it level (when I took the heads off some of the wood came off on some of them). Then started gluing the tops on those without filler. Finished gluing tops on Thursday afternoon. Today I cleaned the glue off and started leveling the seam, I got as far as 3. I also have to redo 2 of them because the new ivory cracked.

Emerson 41109: Finished. Hallet Davis 31849: Did stringing prep - the agraffes were a bit of a puzzle. I messaged that I had a question about them but I think I have them figured out. There are two versions of every bridge agraffe, with the string angling up or down through the agraffe. I'm doing every other one, thinking that it's to equalize any pull the string is putting in one direction in the bridge. You can see if you look on the bass bridge agraffes easily.

Tu: did the post-pitch raise tuning super quick, got case parts on, helped Katrina some more, helped Matthew with stringing set up, made lots of progress on Erard hammers. The bandsaw hogs off a lot of material (as illegal as it feels) and makes filing to shape a lot easier. The staples are starting to really get in the way, and I've been struggling to get the hammers to look decent after filing. I'm probably just being picky so I haven't been spending any time trying to make them look perfect. W: more erard hammer progress, warranty repinning. Th: Almost finished Erard hammers. F: The Milton had a TON of clicking, and despite trying everything else it seems like the only fix was replacing the butt leather and jack felt. All of the clicking ones were replaced, hammers are almost all back on. It will need letoff and lost motion checked after the action is back in and hopefully the clicking is gone. If the clicks are gone I should be done in 1-2 hours of work, if it's still clicking I'm not really sure what to do. Crown 8770: fixed dampers as best I could. The piano is more on the ringy/echoey side but I think this is the best it can do (the piano). Trapwork needed some work: centered sustain pedal, added some extra felt, lubricated, tightened some loose screws, couldn’t find the rest rail rod so I just found a replacement and adjusted it to the piano, glued some cracks, re-engineered the muffler rail, added bushings to the holes where the muffler rail bars went through, fixed muffler rail system (left bar was supposed to be on the right and viceversa), strengthened the muffler rails trapwork spring, replaced one of its screws that had broken, replaced muffler felt on the rail, regulated trapwork. Gave it another pitch raise and a tuning, while tuning I was also paying attention for any QC work: 12 hammers need to be repined, 1 bass damper isn’t pulling back enough from the string and it’s interfering with the reverb, and there’s a click on B3 (when returning), and G2 is sounding a bit darker than it’s neighbors. I also noticed that some of the last octave notes sound kinda dead and I figured out that the lip on the plate up there actually curves down, so C8 sounds good at it’s height but then, towards the left, some of the hammers are hitting that plate lip a bit more because it curves down a bit. It’s super weird, first time I’ve seen anything like it. I don’t think it’s worth doing anything about it but thought you’d might wanna take a look because it’s weird. I think I need one more day on this to finish it.

Chickering 109841: was trying to regulate the section ends when I noticed most of the let off buttons didn’t line up with the jack toes (specially in the second section); this probably has something to do with the changes related to the whippen flanges. The whippens are already aligned to the knuckles and the hammers are alrea aligned to the strings. Also the buttons couldn’t go down far enough. This unchained a series of unfortunate events for me in the shop that haunted me throughout the entire week. First I decided to replace the felts with thicker ones but was told by two coworkers that it was impossible to regulate with those because they’re too squishy, so I then decided to move the entire rail down, make a notch in the action “stands” where it would slide in as its original design, make the notches on the rail a bit deeper so that the stands weren’t left too thin. In one occasion I left the rest rail leaning on the wall and it fell over, splitting the rail in 2. I tried regluing it but it didn’t really work so I decided to move forward with the second part of my plan and deal with it later. I filled in with epoxy the holes of the eye screws on the rail so that I could re-drill them so that they would align with the toes. The next day I re-drilled the holes and installed the let off buttons, tried glueing the rail itself together again with epoxy and left it drying over night, the next day I found it still wet (I guess it needed more hardener?) as I was inspecting it one half fell off the table (I have a very narrow workspace with the keybed on the table) and it shattered into another 3 pieces, all breaks were at the thin notch points for the screws. After this I took a small break to breath it out and decided to build a new rail, this was on Thursday. Finished drilling the holes on the new rail Thursday afternoon, on Friday a coworker accidentally walked into the rail as I was installing the eyescrews and it cracked but not all the way through and was able to glue it and reinforce it with veneer (it worked on this one), finished installing the eyescrews and let off buttons, one of the 88 was a bit loose so I coated the hole with some wood glue and water, leaving it to dry over the weekend. Will reinstall the let off rail on Monday.

It took me a minute to get the hide glue going but I managed to get it working, for now to me the best way to get the hide glue going has been filling up the wax warmer with water about half way or 1/3 of the way and then heating up the water to 80°C, Then making a mix 1:1 ratio of water and hide glue crystal inside some sort of container. Also I think it's good when applying on a surface, to do it in small badges and then returning the container with the mix back to the warmer since the mix cools down decently fast. The gluing is going pretty well regardless, most of deck 1 is glued and cuted

Chickering 109841: I’m regulating by section. Finished regulation on section one, just finished regulating let-off on section 2. I’ve had to do some small fixes as I go: the lower end of the hammer shanks in the bass are slightly wider than the originals an so some of the knuckles were rubbing and getting caught on the neighboring rep-lever, in those cases I sanded the side of the rep-lever a little bit and cut some of the side of the knuckle and sanded. I’m keeping an eye on the bass section though because I had to go through leveling the hammer height a couple of times because it would change, I checked the spring strength and it’s great. Is there any other factor that could cause inconsistent hammer height? I also replaced a drop screw that had a broken slot, I expect to replace another one when I find it, I remember there were 2 broken ones.

Only worked MON & TUES, Kranich & Bach 37121:  new felts on key bed, removed hammers, tumbled much of the very rusty action hardware, soda blasted action, now installing new hammer butts (which have to be repinned onto the existing flange), also helped train Nathaniel on regulation.

Chickering 109841: the height inconsistencies were due to “pining” (only one swing) but it wasn’t because of the pin size, the problem was the part were the shank meets the brass flange was too thick and rubbing a lot with the flange and getting stuck. I fixed these by filing inside the area around the pin hole on the shank and also filing the sides of the flange (after this they had 4-5+ swings). I think I had to do it on all of them by the end, I just did it as I was regulating. I’m almost done regulating, since the height wasn’t consistent I went through refining height after fixing the flanges. I have the third and fourth section to refine. I’ll go through dip/aftertouch again in the piano, still need to regulate dip/aftertouch on the sharps. It’s going great besides that, it’s just a regulation with some extra hurdles. But I really am moving as fast as I can when I’m working on my project. My most productive day this week was Monday (I was in the shop all day). I saw that I already have 3 tunings next week. May I request no more tunings scheduled for next week? I really want to meet your expectations as best I can.

Turns out, though, that I'm probably not lifting the pedal up all the way between chord changes. (Same thing was happening when I played a few pianos in the showroom.) If I test individual chords and lift the pedal up all the way, it's okay.
I think the piano could use a bit of voicing in the mid/high treble, mostly around E5 to G7 or so.  When I play, that range of notes overpowers the rest of the piano, and it's difficult to play softly up there.

A heads up, I'll be in Durango, CO (where my grandpa grew up), from Sept 18 (Wed) to 24 (Tue), with my parents.  (I'll be meeting them at the airport there around 12:30pm so I'll want to take at least half or all of Sept 17 (Tue) off to get ready.  They're scheduled to fly out around 11:30am the 24th (Tue), but I may want to be prepared to maybe not come in the 25th (Wed, or maybe in the afternoon) in case things come up.


Several white keys (and some adjacent black keys) needed sanding or in at least one case un-warping (thanks Adeline!), still working on spacing them, and to some extent squaring.  (Sometimes I had to temporarily remove an adjacent interfering key to space its neighbor.)

I noticed one (D6, #66) has its keytop glued on slightly crooked, was told it's not bad enough to worry about.

As I was finalizing the setup for the black key leveling, I noticed the keys are quite high on the front rail pins, like just BARELY hanging on, with the adjacent white key gone I could easily knock the key off the pin with a sideways push.  I have the white keys set at about 20mm (about 0.7874", Adeline likes metric) above the key slip, and those are a bit high on the front rail pins too, not quite as bad, as Victor Borge called them, as the civil rights. (As I said earlier, I'm still working on setting up the cracks properly.)

I was shown another jig for setting sharp height than I was starting with, so I'm using that. (It wasn't in the punchings box I was using.) Hopefully it works okay, although I still think the keys are kinda high on the front rail pins.

A few keys at the back end were cracked and needed to be reglued.  I noticed on #13 (A1), the capstan screw is awfully close to the left edge, but is centered under the sticker toe (or whatever it's called on the Thayer inverted sticker action in this Acrosonic.)  The sticker itself is pretty much centered on the capstan, but slightly left relative to its neighbors (but not much I see can be done about that on a refurb, because of the guide pin holes not allowing for much leeway in adjustment.)  Also several keys are really close to the sticker toes to the right, with #12 (G#1) sometimes lifting #13 (A1), and same with #14 (A#1) vs #15 (B1).

Also, #9 (F1) is sitting a bit high, being interfered with by the balance rail punchings under #10 (F#1).

Now if I knew how to condense a Friday report without leaving things out…

Noticed some keys binding on each other, and some loose keytops, so have been doing a lot of gluing and some sanding / filing of keytops this week. (Some were interfering with neighbors and/or overhanging too far.)  Got a front bushing replaced (was mangled; Gracias, Marcelo, y por enseñarme, Ahora, si tan solo pudiera recordarlo la próxima vez que necesite hacerlo.), Haven't checked other bushings yet, they feel a little loose with the wobble test but I'm still able to play the piano.

I noticed the sharps were high (practically popping off the front rail pins with a bit of sideways pressure, as mentioned last week).  Ended up lowering them about 0.020” (some more or less to get them all level).

Also squared and spaced keys, except a few around #78 (D#7) which is getting is keytop reglued.  It might have been ready later this afternoon but last time I tried sanding/filing a keytop too soon after gluing it popped off so I plan to do that Monday.  Also I hope to get started with the next part of regulation, which I believe is setting blow distance.  (The drop stickers may limit how far I can move the backchecks back; also I noticed earlier when I had the keyslip screwed in that some keys were binding on the front so I may need to address that.  And sometimes the jack for #39 (B3) parks under the hammer butt in a way to push the hammer prematurely, making it harder to reinstall the key.  (The bridle wire is pretty far back, I think that’s one we’d replaced a while ago.)  Also there’s a felt block sitting on top of the piano, I believe that’s the one that keeps the rest rail from going too far forward when using the soft pedal.

Speaking of Monday, that’s the only day I’ll be in next week, as I’m going to Colorado on Wednesday, leaving early in the morning (parents get into Durango airport at 12:34pm) and I’ll be getting ready Tuesday (and some before when I’m not at work).  I expect to be back Thursday the 26th.  (I may be back in town the 25 or late the 24th, but I’m leaving it open in case things come up, for example issues with my parents’ flight out of Durango, CO.)

Baldwin Acrosonic #402277 - Continued with / finished backcheck regulation. Regulated dip, noticed it's fairly shallow (had been very deep, used a lot of punchings) but was told it's okay (also the older Acrosonic in the showroom has shallow dip.  I'm still kind of wondering if the key height is maybe a bit high, as I had to use a ton of punchings there and make major adjustments to other regulation steps, including short blow distance, but it's set where I was told to put it.)
Adjusted some spoons that were lifting way too early and throwing off regulation of damper lift.
Regulating the damper lift has been a bit frustrating to put it nicely.  Learning to find the spot to put the tool is one thing, then I'm always either not making a difference or going way too far.  (It does now look just about as good as Acrosonic 343593, but nowhere near as good as any of the Hailun or Yamaha uprights, or Baldwin 66020, Hailun 258357.)
Resting my foot on the pedal lets most notes ring (except a few dampers that set their alarm clock for PM instead of AM), but I was told the pedal already has plenty of lost motion (or was it too much).
Also there are several other notes that have issues besides the ones I mentioned last week, including 54, 66, 2-15, 7, 6, 32, 34. Also loose hammer heads on 72, 63, 64, 76, 79, 84, 85, 86, possibly a couple dozen others.  I probably need to pull the action to make some repairs (was advised to keep / finish regulating first as best I could).
Hearing a squeak or creak in the sustain pedal

Baldwin Acrosonic #402277 - SO close to being done, except I hit a snag. (I was really wanting to complete it today.) The hinge on the (back of the) music desk is bent (and was partly removed), and when I tried to put it back together, it wouldn't open and close properly.  I found a new one to replace it, but no matter which way I put it on, it won't work properly.  It looks like maybe if I could reverse one side of the hinge it might work, but I (for now) lack the proper experience to do it right. (I tried a couple things but that wasn't working.)  

Otherwise, I just need to put the top lid and bottom board back on, and it's done.  (Well, it could use about a 30-40+¢ pitch raise and tuning, although I think considering it's a spinet I kinda like the voicing as it is, I'd like to play it some after it's tuned to be sure.)

Maybe my hammer technique needs adjustment.)
